stem:
A 5-month-old boy is admitted to a regional paediatric ward with 3 days of coryza followed by 24 hours of laboured breathing and reduced feeding. He is taking about 50% of usual milk volumes and has wet nappies 4 hourly. He was born at 38 weeks gestation with no perinatal complications and has no chronic conditions.

On arrival he is alert with moderate subcostal and intercostal recession, widespread fine inspiratory crackles, and scattered wheeze. Nasopharyngeal aspirate returns positive for respiratory syncytial virus. He has been on room air for the first 4 hours, with SpO2 fluctuating between 88% and 91%. Low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 L/min has been running for the past 2 hours and SpO2 is now persistently 89%-90% with ongoing moderate work of breathing.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 168/min, BP 88/52 mmHg, RR 62/min, SpO2 90% on 1 L/min nasal prong, temperature 37.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Moderate subcostal and intercostal recession, no head bobbing, no apnoeas; bilateral crackles and wheeze; capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Capillary blood gas pH 7.34, pCO2 48 mmHg, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, lactate 1.8 mmol/L (0.5-2.2); nasopharyngeal aspirate RSV positive; chest x-ray hyperinflation only

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in respiratory support?

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min with heated humidified oxygen titrated to maintain SpO2 at 90% or above
options.rationale: RCH defines low-flow failure as ongoing moderate work of breathing or SpO2 below 90% after 2 hours on maximal low-flow; HFNC at 2 L/kg/min is the next step in escalation in the ward. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Increase low-flow nasal prong oxygen from 1 L/min to 4 L/min through the standard nasal prongs and continue monitoring in the ward
options.rationale: Standard nasal prongs deliver up to 2 L/min in infants under 12 months per RCH; flows above this are uncomfortable, dry the airway, and are not part of the bronchiolitis escalation pathway. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Continue low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 L/min and reassess work of breathing and saturations again in another 2 hours
options.rationale: After 2 hours on low-flow with persistent saturations below the 90% target and ongoing moderate work of breathing, continuing the same therapy meets the RCH definition of low-flow failure and risks delayed escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer a 5 mg salbutamol nebuliser and reassess respiratory effort and oxygen requirement 20 minutes after the dose has finished
options.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended for bronchiolitis under 12 months and do not change outcomes or oxygen requirement; the wheeze reflects small-airway plugging, not bronchospasm. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Switch directly to bilevel positive airway pressure via tight-fitting face mask in the high dependency area for ongoing respiratory support
options.rationale: BiPAP bypasses the HFNC step in the RCH escalation ladder; HFNC failure (rising pCO2, rising RR despite 2 L/kg/min, exhaustion) is the trigger to escalate to non-invasive ventilation in PICU. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: RCH bronchiolitis pathway escalates respiratory support in steps: room air, then low-flow nasal prong oxygen (1-2 L/min for infants), then high-flow nasal cannula (2 L/kg/min heated humidified) on the ward, then non-invasive ventilation in PICU. The SpO2 target is 90% or above on minimal support. Bronchodilators, nebulised hypertonic saline, systemic corticosteroids, and routine chest physiotherapy are not recommended for bronchiolitis. Apnoeas, exhaustion, or rising pCO2 are PICU-level triggers.
key_points: SpO2 target is 90% (not 92-94%) in bronchiolitis under RCH
Low-flow oxygen via nasal prongs is capped at 2 L/min in infants
HFNC 2 L/kg/min is the standard ward-level escalation step
Bronchodilators have no role in bronchiolitis under 12 months
Apnoeas, rising pCO2, or HFNC failure trigger PICU transfer
explanation.pearls: Low-flow nasal prong oxygen flow rate in infants is 2 L/min maximum; if persistent SpO2 under 90% or moderate work of breathing after 2 hours, escalate to HFNC at 2 L/kg/min.

stem:
A 13-month-old girl is brought to a paediatric emergency department 15 minutes after a single bite of a peanut butter sandwich at home. The parents report rapid onset of facial swelling, widespread red blotchy rash on the trunk, persistent harsh barking cough, and one episode of vomiting on the way to hospital. She has known egg and dairy allergy but no previous reaction to peanut.

In the resuscitation cubicle she is alert but distressed with audible inspiratory stridor and bilateral wheeze. A cot weight is unavailable but a recent weighing card states 11 kg. Intravenous access has not yet been established. The nurse has the paediatric anaphylaxis tray open and is asking which adrenaline dose to draw up.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 172/min, BP 78/40 mmHg, RR 48/min, SpO2 93% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Lip and periorbital oedema, urticarial rash trunk and limbs; inspiratory stridor with bilateral wheeze; capillary refill 3 seconds; not yet cannulated
data_table.Investigations: Bedside investigations deferred pending resuscitation; weight on weighing card 11 kg (no current cot weight)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate single best initial adrenaline dose and route?

options.text: Adrenaline 110 micrograms (0.11 mg of 1:1000) intramuscular into the anterolateral mid-thigh, repeated every 5 minutes if no response
options.rationale: Calculating 10 mcg/kg arithmetic from a non-current weight is the historical method that ASCIA replaced; the per-kg formula is no longer the preferred prescribing approach in the emergency setting. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis

options.text: Adrenaline 50 micrograms (0.5 mL of 1:10 000) slow intravenous bolus over 5 minutes after rapid IV cannulation has been established
options.rationale: IV adrenaline is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis after at least 2 IM doses and only with cardiac monitoring; first-line is always IM into the anterolateral thigh. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis

options.text: Adrenaline 300 micrograms (0.30 mg of 1:1000) intramuscular into the anterolateral mid-thigh, repeated every 5 minutes if no response
options.rationale: 300 mcg (EpiPen) is the ASCIA banded dose for children 20 kg or more (approximately 6 years and older), not for an 11 kg toddler; overdose risks tachyarrhythmia in small children. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis

options.text: Adrenaline 150 micrograms (0.15 mg of 1:1000) intramuscular into the anterolateral mid-thigh, repeated every 5 minutes if no response
options.rationale: The ASCIA banded table assigns 150 mcg IM adrenaline to children 1 to under 6 years (approximately 10-20 kg), independent of exact weight; matches an EpiPen Jr. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis

options.text: Adrenaline 5 mg nebulised (5 mL of 1:1000) via face mask immediately to treat the upper airway oedema and the audible stridor
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline can supplement upper airway oedema management AFTER IM adrenaline; it does not address systemic anaphylaxis and is never the first single best initial intervention. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis

explanation.summary: ASCIA recommends a banded weight-and-age table for IM adrenaline in paediatric anaphylaxis to remove calculation error in resuscitation: under 1 year (under 10 kg) 100 mcg, 1 to under 6 years (10-20 kg) 150 mcg, 6 to under 12 years (20-50 kg) 300 mcg, 12 years or older (over 50 kg) 500 mcg. The IM site is the anterolateral mid-thigh. Repeat every 5 minutes if no response. IV adrenaline is reserved for refractory cases after 2 IM doses with cardiac monitoring.
key_points: ASCIA paediatric IM adrenaline uses banded doses, not per-kg arithmetic
10-20 kg child receives 150 mcg IM (EpiPen Jr equivalent)
Anterolateral mid-thigh is the only IM site
Repeat at 5 minute intervals if persistent symptoms
IV adrenaline only after 2 failed IM doses with cardiac monitoring
explanation.pearls: 150 mcg IM adrenaline is the ASCIA dose for any child 10-20 kg; the banded table is the AU standard precisely because resuscitation is not the moment for mg/kg arithmetic.

sources.label: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis

stem:
A 22-month-old boy is brought to a metropolitan emergency department with 36 hours of profuse non-bloody diarrhoea and 24 hours of vomiting up to 6 times daily. His older brother had similar symptoms last week. Parents report he is taking small sips of water but vomiting most of them.

He is alert but irritable on assessment. He weighs 12 kg today; the family Red Book records 12.7 kg three weeks ago. Eyes appear sunken, mouth is dry, capillary refill is 2 to 3 seconds, peripheries are warm. He has had no wet nappy for 6 hours. A first dose of oral ondansetron 2 mg was given on triage 30 minutes ago and he has just vomited again. He has no abdominal distension, no peritonism, no bilious vomit, and no blood in the stool.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 142/min, BP 92/60 mmHg, RR 28/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 37.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, soft non-tender abdomen, no organomegaly; capillary refill 2-3 seconds; weight loss 5.5%
data_table.Investigations: Capillary blood gas pH 7.34, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L, lactate 2.1 mmol/L (0.5-2.2); urea and electrolytes within normal limits; venous glucose 4.6 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in rehydration?

options.text: Encourage small frequent oral sips of clear fluids at home and review with the general practitioner in the next 24 to 48 hours for clinical reassessment
options.rationale: Discharge home is inappropriate when oral intake has just failed in the department with a documented 5.5% weight loss and ongoing vomiting after ondansetron. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Insert an intravenous cannula and administer a 20 mL/kg normal saline bolus over the next 30 minutes followed by maintenance intravenous fluids
options.rationale: IV rehydration is reserved for severe dehydration, shock, NGT failure, or significant electrolyte derangement; NGT is preferred for moderate dehydration with normal electrolytes and no shock. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Insert a nasogastric tube and commence oral rehydration solution at 25 mL/kg over the first hour followed by ongoing maintenance plus deficit
options.rationale: RCH first-line for moderate dehydration after failed oral intake is NGT rehydration with ORS; faster, safer, and equally effective as IV in moderate dehydration without shock. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Repeat ondansetron 4 mg orally and reassess oral fluid tolerance again in 30 minutes before considering any further intervention or escalation
options.rationale: Ondansetron is a single dose only in paediatric gastroenteritis and the dose for 8-15 kg is 2 mg; repeated dosing is not recommended and delays definitive rehydration. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Send urgent stool sample for microscopy culture sensitivity and rotavirus PCR before commencing any active rehydration to confirm the underlying cause
options.rationale: Stool studies are not routine in uncomplicated viral gastroenteritis and do not change acute management; rehydration is the priority and is not stool-result dependent. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

explanation.summary: Australian paediatric practice prefers NGT rehydration over IV for moderate dehydration without shock because it is faster to start, less invasive, has fewer complications, and matches outcomes of IV therapy. A single dose of oral ondansetron (2 mg for 8-15 kg, 4 mg for 15-30 kg) is recommended for vomiting; repeat doses are not. IV is reserved for severe dehydration, shock, NGT failure, decreased conscious state, or significant electrolyte/acid-base derangement. Routine stool studies and antibiotics are not indicated in uncomplicated viral gastroenteritis.
key_points: NGT rehydration is first-line for moderate dehydration without shock
Ondansetron is single-dose only: 2 mg (8-15 kg) or 4 mg (15-30 kg)
IV reserved for severe dehydration, shock, or NGT failure
Routine stool studies do not change acute management
Antibiotics are not indicated for uncomplicated viral gastroenteritis
explanation.pearls: NGT ORS at 25 mL/kg over the first hour is the RCH protocol for moderate dehydration after failed oral intake; IV access is needed only if NGT fails or shock develops.

subtopic_detail: Incomplete Kawasaki disease in an infant
stem:
A 7-month-old girl is reviewed on day 7 of a febrile illness in a regional paediatric ward. Persistent daily fevers to 39.5 °C have not responded to paracetamol or ibuprofen; oral amoxicillin started by the GP on day 3 has made no difference. She has been miserable, off feeds, and her mother reports erythema of the hands which has now begun to peel around the fingertips.

On examination she has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, a faint perineal rash, and a single 1.5 cm cervical lymph node. Lips are red but not fissured. There is no oropharyngeal exudate. Three of the five classical Kawasaki criteria are present. Inflammatory markers are markedly elevated and a transaminitis is noted; urine has sterile pyuria. A focused echocardiogram has been arranged and is pending.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 158/min, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 36/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 39.2 °C
data_table.Examination: Conjunctival injection bilateral non-exudative, single 1.5 cm cervical node, perineal rash, peeling fingertips; no joint swelling; mild hepatomegaly
data_table.Investigations: CRP 145 mg/L (<5), ESR 78 mm/h, WCC 18.4 x10^9/L with neutrophilia, platelets 612 x10^9/L, ALT 96 U/L (<35); urine microscopy 80 x10^6/L leucocytes with negative culture; albumin 28 g/L (35-50)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Wait for completion of the echocardiogram and only commence treatment if coronary artery z-score is greater than 2.5 on the formal report
options.rationale: Treatment of suspected Kawasaki disease is not contingent on a coronary abnormality; delaying IVIG beyond day 10 of illness markedly increases the risk of coronary aneurysm. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Continue supportive care alone and reassess clinically in 48 hours given that classical criteria for Kawasaki disease are not yet fully met
options.rationale: Infants under 12 months are at highest risk of coronary aneurysms and most often present with incomplete criteria; AHA and RCH explicitly recommend treating incomplete Kawasaki on supportive laboratory features. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 10 to 12 hours together with high-dose aspirin pending the formal echocardiogram report
options.rationale: Incomplete Kawasaki in an infant with 3 of 5 criteria plus elevated CRP, transaminitis, sterile pyuria, hypoalbuminaemia and thrombocytosis meets RCH IVIG criteria; treat within 10 days of fever onset. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Switch oral amoxicillin to intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily for empirical cover of an unidentified bacterial focus before any other intervention
options.rationale: Persistent fever for 7 days with documented sterile pyuria, no localising focus, and Kawasaki features makes broadened antibiotics inappropriate as a primary intervention; the diagnosis is Kawasaki until proven otherwise. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Commence a 3-day course of intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg/day as primary therapy and review the response after the third pulse
options.rationale: Pulse steroids are reserved for IVIG-resistant Kawasaki disease or selected high-risk cases (Kobayashi score, infants under 12 months may be considered for adjunctive steroid); they are NOT primary monotherapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is the rule in infants and a leading cause of acquired coronary artery disease in children. AHA/RCH supplementary laboratory criteria (CRP over 30 mg/L plus 3 of: anaemia for age, platelets over 450 after day 7, albumin under 30 g/L, ALT elevation, WCC over 15, sterile pyuria) support empirical IVIG when fewer than 4 of 5 classical features are present. IVIG 2 g/kg with aspirin should be given within 10 days of fever onset. Steroids are adjunctive in IVIG-resistant disease or high-risk infants; echocardiography is performed to baseline coronary status but does not gate initial treatment.
key_points: Treat incomplete Kawasaki on supportive lab criteria, do not wait for echo
IVIG within 10 days of fever onset reduces aneurysm risk to about 5%
Infants under 12 months have the highest aneurysm risk
Sterile pyuria, transaminitis, hypoalbuminaemia, thrombocytosis are supportive
Pulse steroids are adjunctive (Kobayashi-high or IVIG-resistant), not primary
explanation.pearls: IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin within day 10 of fever onset cuts coronary aneurysm risk from around 25% to under 5%; infants under 12 months should be treated on lower thresholds because incomplete presentation is the norm.

sources.label: AHA 2017 Scientific Statement Diagnosis Treatment and Long-Term Management of Kawasaki Disease

stem:
A 5-year-old boy with newly diagnosed B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukaemia presents to a metropolitan paediatric emergency department on day 10 of induction chemotherapy. The mother brings him in because of a single fever to 38.6 °C tympanic and a brief shaking chill. He has a tunnelled central venous line in place that looks clean and is non-tender.

He appears tired but alert, with capillary refill of 2 seconds and warm peripheries. There is no oral mucositis, no abdominal tenderness, no perianal tenderness, and no focal infective findings on examination. The leukaemia treatment team has been notified. Blood cultures have been drawn from both line lumens and a peripheral site. Empirical antibiotic choice is being prepared in the resuscitation cubicle.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 122/min, BP 102/60 mmHg, RR 24/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 38.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Central line site clean and non-tender, no oral mucositis, soft abdomen, no perianal tenderness, no skin lesions
data_table.Investigations: Neutrophil count 0.3 x10^9/L, platelets 42 x10^9/L, haemoglobin 92 g/L; CRP 65 mg/L; venous lactate 1.6 mmol/L (0.5-2.2); chest x-ray clear; urine microscopy bland; blood cultures pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical intravenous antibiotic regimen for this child?

options.text: Intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam 100 mg/kg per dose every 6 hours as monotherapy after blood cultures have been drawn from line and peripheral sites
options.rationale: ChildKidsCAN and eviQ paediatric febrile neutropenia guidance recommends piperacillin-tazobactam monotherapy as empirical first-line in haemodynamically stable children without focal severe infection or known resistant organism. (Source: ChildKidsCAN Paediatric Febrile Neutropenia Guideline; eviQ)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily plus oral amoxicillin twice daily for empirical streptococcal and gram-negative cover until cultures are reported
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone has no antipseudomonal activity and oral amoxicillin is inappropriate in a febrile neutropenic patient; AU paediatric guidance requires antipseudomonal IV cover empirically. (Source: ChildKidsCAN Paediatric Febrile Neutropenia Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 15 mg/kg every 6 hours and intravenous gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg once daily without any beta-lactam pending final blood culture results
options.rationale: Empirical vancomycin is reserved for shock, line infection, severe mucositis, MRSA-colonised patients, or gram-positive growth; routine empirical vancomycin is not indicated and aminoglycoside monotherapy is never appropriate. (Source: ChildKidsCAN Paediatric Febrile Neutropenia Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous meropenem 20 mg/kg every 8 hours immediately as empirical broad-spectrum cover and add filgrastim 5 mcg/kg subcutaneously on the day of admission
options.rationale: Meropenem is reserved for prior ESBL colonisation, recent broad-spectrum exposure, or critical illness; first-line stable febrile neutropenia is piperacillin-tazobactam. Routine G-CSF is not recommended empirically. (Source: ChildKidsCAN Paediatric Febrile Neutropenia Guideline)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily combined with oral ciprofloxacin discharge home with daily outpatient haematology phone review
options.rationale: Australian paediatric practice admits ALL children with febrile neutropenia for IV antibiotics; outpatient oral regimens for low-risk febrile neutropenia exist in adult/adolescent care but not as default in induction-phase paediatric ALL. (Source: ChildKidsCAN Paediatric Febrile Neutropenia Guideline)

explanation.summary: Paediatric febrile neutropenia (single tympanic temperature 38.5 °C or above OR two readings 38.0 °C an hour apart with neutrophils under 0.5 x10^9/L) is a medical emergency requiring blood cultures and IV antipseudomonal beta-lactam within 60 minutes. Piperacillin-tazobactam is the AU first-line empirical choice; add vancomycin only for shock, line tunnel infection, severe mucositis, MRSA colonisation, or gram-positive growth. Meropenem is reserved for ESBL, recent broad-spectrum failure, or critical illness. Routine G-CSF is not indicated. ALL induction patients are admitted regardless of risk score.
key_points: Empirical piperacillin-tazobactam monotherapy is AU first-line for stable paediatric febrile neutropenia
Antibiotic should be in within 60 minutes of arrival
Vancomycin add-on for shock, line, severe mucositis, MRSA, or gram-positive growth
Meropenem reserved for ESBL or critical illness
Induction-phase paediatric ALL patients always admitted for IV therapy
explanation.pearls: Piperacillin-tazobactam 100 mg/kg per dose 6-hourly within 60 minutes of arrival is the AU paediatric febrile neutropenia first-line; door-to-antibiotic time is the auditable quality metric.

subtopic_detail: Hypercyanotic spell in tetralogy of Fallot
stem:
A 10-month-old girl with known unrepaired tetralogy of Fallot is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after a brief episode at home of inconsolable crying, deepening lip colour, and limpness. She has previously had two milder spells which resolved as her mother brought her knees to her chest. The cardiology team is aware and a primary repair is planned for next month.

In the resuscitation bay she is irritable but rousable. SpO2 has been 72% to 78% on room air for the last 10 minutes despite knee-to-chest positioning by the parent and 8 L/min facemask oxygen. She has a barely audible right upper sternal edge systolic murmur. Peripheries are cool and dusky. Capillary refill is 4 seconds centrally. Intravenous access has been established. Cardiology has been called and is en route.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 184/min, BP 78/44 mmHg, RR 56/min, SpO2 76% on 8 L/min mask, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Central cyanosis, knees drawn up to chest, soft systolic ejection murmur (previously grade 3, now grade 1), cool peripheries, capillary refill 4 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Venous blood gas pH 7.18, lactate 5.8 mmol/L (0.5-2.2), bicarbonate 14 mmol/L; haemoglobin 168 g/L; venous glucose 5.2 mmol/L; bedside echo deferred to inpatient team

lead_in: After positioning, oxygen, and intravenous fluid bolus, what is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Intravenous prostaglandin E1 infusion at 0.05 micrograms/kg/min to keep the ductus arteriosus open and improve oxygenated pulmonary blood flow at this age
options.rationale: The ductus closes within days of birth and is not reopenable at 10 months of age; PGE1 has no role in hypercyanotic spells in an older infant with established tetralogy. (Source: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot Hypercyanotic Spell)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot Hypercyanotic Spell

options.text: Intravenous adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg push as inotropic support given the cool peripheries and the prolonged centralised capillary refill time of 4 seconds
options.rationale: Catecholamines increase infundibular contraction and worsen right ventricular outflow obstruction; adrenaline is contraindicated in unrepaired tetralogy of Fallot hypercyanotic spells. (Source: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot Hypercyanotic Spell)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot Hypercyanotic Spell

options.text: Intravenous morphine 0.1 mg/kg by slow injection to reduce hyperpnoea and break the spiral of right ventricular outflow tract spasm and cyanosis
options.rationale: After knee-to-chest, oxygen, and IV fluids, morphine 0.05-0.1 mg/kg IV reduces hyperpnoea and infundibular spasm and is the recommended next step in unbroken hypercyanotic spells. (Source: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot Hypercyanotic Spell; Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot Hypercyanotic Spell

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 1 mg/kg to offload the right ventricle and reduce pulmonary congestion before the cardiology team arrives at the bedside
options.rationale: Hypercyanotic spells reflect right-to-left shunt with reduced pulmonary blood flow, not pulmonary congestion; diuretics drop systemic vascular resistance and preload and worsen the spell. (Source: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot Hypercyanotic Spell)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot Hypercyanotic Spell

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg as an immediate buffer for the metabolic acidosis demonstrated on the bedside venous blood gas analysis
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is reserved for refractory acidosis after positioning, oxygen, fluid, morphine and phenylephrine have failed; it is not the next step ahead of morphine which addresses the mechanism. (Source: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot Hypercyanotic Spell)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot Hypercyanotic Spell

explanation.summary: Hypercyanotic spells in tetralogy of Fallot arise from acute right ventricular outflow tract infundibular spasm with worsened right-to-left shunt across the VSD. The sequenced approach is knee-to-chest positioning (raises systemic vascular resistance), supplemental oxygen, and IV fluid bolus to increase preload. Morphine 0.05-0.1 mg/kg IV reduces hyperpnoea and infundibular spasm. Phenylephrine boluses (1-5 mcg/kg) raise systemic vascular resistance for refractory cases. Adrenaline is contraindicated because it worsens infundibular contraction; PGE1 has no role beyond the neonatal period.
key_points: Sequence: positioning, oxygen, IV fluids, morphine, then phenylephrine
Morphine 0.05-0.1 mg/kg IV reduces hyperpnoea and infundibular spasm
Adrenaline is contraindicated; phenylephrine raises SVR without infundibular spasm
PGE1 has no role after the ductus has closed in older infants
Refractory spells require intubation, paralysis, and urgent cardiology
explanation.pearls: In a hypercyanotic spell, the audible murmur quietens because right-to-left shunt has bypassed the RVOT; a previously loud murmur going silent is a marker of severity, not improvement.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot Hypercyanotic Spell

stem:
A 4-week-old boy is reviewed at a paediatric outpatient clinic for severe constipation since birth. The mother reports he did not pass any stool until day 3 of life when a paediatric resident performed a digital examination at the maternity ward, which produced a forceful explosion of meconium. Since then he opens his bowels only every 4 to 5 days with marked straining, and only after a glycerine suppository.

He is bottle fed with standard formula and is on the 25th centile for weight (down from the 50th at birth). The abdomen is mildly distended without visible bowel loops. There is no inguinal hernia and the perianal area looks normal. A focused examination shows a tight anus with an explosive release of gas and watery stool after the examining finger is removed. Plain abdominal x-ray shows dilated bowel loops with absent rectal gas.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 140/min, BP 78/48 mmHg, RR 38/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Mild abdominal distension, soft non-tender abdomen, tight anus with explosive release of gas and stool on rectal examination; weight 25th centile, length 50th centile
data_table.Investigations: Plain abdominal x-ray dilated proximal bowel with reduced rectal gas; haemoglobin 132 g/L, electrolytes within normal limits; thyroid stimulating hormone 2.4 mIU/L (0.4-4.0); coeliac serology not yet sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the underlying diagnosis?

options.text: Trial of macrogol osmotic laxative for 4 weeks at maintenance dose and review the response in clinic before considering invasive testing or referral
options.rationale: Empirical laxative trial is appropriate for functional constipation but not for an infant with delayed meconium passage and explosive release on PR; functional constipation rarely begins in the first month of life. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Suction rectal biopsy at the appropriate level above the dentate line obtained at a paediatric surgical centre for histopathology
options.rationale: Delayed meconium passage beyond 48 hours plus chronic obstructive constipation with explosive release on digital examination is Hirschsprung disease until proven otherwise; suction rectal biopsy is the gold-standard diagnostic test. (Source: RACGP red book Paediatrics; RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Sweat chloride test conducted on at least two occasions to exclude cystic fibrosis as the underlying cause for the meconium plug and constipation
options.rationale: Meconium ileus from cystic fibrosis usually presents in the first 48 hours with bilious vomiting and failure to pass meconium, not chronic explosive release on PR; this patient already has newborn screen and clinical features pointing to Hirschsprung. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Anorectal manometry to demonstrate the absence of the rectoanal inhibitory reflex via balloon distension under conscious sedation by paediatric gastroenterology
options.rationale: Anorectal manometry is technically difficult in young infants and is a screening test in older children; AU practice goes directly to suction rectal biopsy for histology in infants with classical features. (Source: RACGP Paediatric Constipation Guidelines)

options.text: Barium enema with full bowel preparation to look for the transition zone on imaging and confirm the diagnosis before progressing to definitive surgical management
options.rationale: Contrast enema can suggest a transition zone but is not diagnostic and has poor sensitivity in neonates; histological confirmation by suction rectal biopsy is required before surgery is offered. (Source: RACGP Paediatric Constipation Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Hirschsprung disease should be suspected in any neonate who has not passed meconium within 48 hours, or in an infant with chronic obstructive constipation, abdominal distension, and explosive release of stool after digital examination. Suction rectal biopsy showing absent ganglion cells and hypertrophied nerve trunks is the diagnostic gold standard. Contrast enema and anorectal manometry are supportive but not diagnostic. Definitive treatment is a pull-through procedure (Soave, Swenson, or Duhamel). Untreated Hirschsprung is complicated by enterocolitis, perforation, and death.
key_points: Failure to pass meconium beyond 48 hours is a red flag for Hirschsprung disease
Explosive release of stool after PR is the classical clinical sign
Suction rectal biopsy is the gold-standard diagnostic test
Contrast enema can suggest but not confirm the diagnosis
Hirschsprung enterocolitis is the leading cause of mortality before surgery
explanation.pearls: Failure to pass meconium beyond 48 hours of life plus explosive release of stool after PR examination triggers suction rectal biopsy referral; the biopsy must be at least 2 cm above the dentate line to avoid the normal hypoganglionic zone.

subtopic_detail: Hyperammonaemia presentation in late-onset urea cycle disorder
stem:
A 2-year-old boy is transferred from a regional emergency department after 12 hours of vomiting and progressive drowsiness. His family had a high-protein BBQ dinner the night before symptoms began. He was previously well except for two unexplained encephalopathic episodes at 9 and 14 months that were attributed to viral gastroenteritis. An older male sibling died unexpectedly at 18 months overseas; cause of death is not documented.

On arrival he opens eyes only to painful stimuli and has hyperventilation without obvious respiratory distress. There is no fever, neck stiffness, or focal neurology. Initial investigations done at the referring hospital show normal sodium, glucose, lactate, ketones, ammonia not measured. A repeat venous blood gas in your department shows respiratory alkalosis. Plasma ammonia returns markedly elevated; lactate is normal; the rest of the metabolic screen is pending.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 132/min, BP 108/68 mmHg, RR 36/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 37.2 °C
data_table.Examination: GCS E2 V2 M4 (8/15), pupils equal and reactive, no focal deficit, no rash, no organomegaly, no signs of trauma
data_table.Investigations: Plasma ammonia 480 micromol/L (<50), arterial blood gas pH 7.52 pCO2 28 mmHg HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L (0.5-2.2), glucose 5.4 mmol/L, urine ketones negative, sodium 138 mmol/L, anion gap normal; CT brain no acute findings

lead_in: Beyond intubation and intensive care transfer, what is the single most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous dextrose 10% at 1.5 times maintenance rate plus intralipid 20% infusion and stop all enteral protein intake while awaiting metabolic team review
options.rationale: Dextrose and lipid stop protein catabolism but do not remove ammonia from the circulation; ammonia at 480 micromol/L is acutely neurotoxic and requires extracorporeal removal alongside the metabolic measures. (Source: BIMDG Hyperammonaemia Protocol; RCH CPG: Metabolic Disorders)

options.text: Give intravenous mannitol 0.5 g/kg over 20 minutes to manage incipient cerebral oedema and continue conservative metabolic management with regular ammonia rechecks
options.rationale: Hyperammonaemia-induced cerebral oedema is treated by removing ammonia, not by osmotic therapy; mannitol does not address the underlying cause and delays definitive treatment. (Source: BIMDG Hyperammonaemia Protocol)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium benzoate 250 mg/kg load followed by sodium phenylbutyrate 250 mg/kg over 90 minutes and reassess ammonia level in 2 hours
options.rationale: Scavengers are essential adjuncts but reduce ammonia too slowly when levels exceed 400 micromol/L; they are used alongside dialysis, not as a standalone strategy at this severity. (Source: BIMDG Hyperammonaemia Protocol)

options.text: Initiate urgent extracorporeal continuous renal replacement therapy via central access at a tertiary centre to remove ammonia mechanically and rapidly
options.rationale: Plasma ammonia above 400 micromol/L with encephalopathy is the threshold for dialysis (haemodialysis preferred, CRRT acceptable); pharmacological scavengers alone cannot drop levels fast enough to avoid permanent neurological injury. (Source: BIMDG Hyperammonaemia Protocol)

options.text: Empirical intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and aciclovir 20 mg/kg pending the lumbar puncture and CSF analysis to exclude a viral or bacterial cause of encephalopathy
options.rationale: Bacterial or viral encephalitis are reasonable differentials but the documented ammonia at 480 micromol/L and respiratory alkalosis pattern point to a urea cycle disorder; antibiotics alone do not treat the metabolic emergency. (Source: BIMDG Hyperammonaemia Protocol)

explanation.summary: Late-presenting urea cycle disorders (ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency is the most common) often manifest after a protein load or catabolic stress. The classical triad is encephalopathy, respiratory alkalosis (ammonia stimulates the central respiratory drive), and absence of acidosis or hypoglycaemia. Plasma ammonia must be measured on a free-flowing sample placed on ice and run immediately. Above 400 micromol/L in a child with encephalopathy, extracorporeal removal (haemodialysis preferred, CRRT acceptable) is required alongside protein restriction, high-calorie dextrose, and intravenous ammonia scavengers (sodium benzoate and phenylbutyrate). Untreated hyperammonaemic encephalopathy carries a high risk of permanent neurological injury.
key_points: Respiratory alkalosis with encephalopathy in a child warrants an ammonia level
Plasma ammonia above 400 micromol/L plus encephalopathy is the threshold for dialysis
Scavengers reduce ammonia slowly; dialysis is the rapid intervention
Protein restriction and high-calorie dextrose stop endogenous protein catabolism
Family history of unexplained sibling death warrants high suspicion
explanation.pearls: Plasma ammonia must be measured on a free-flowing venous sample placed immediately on ice; falsely elevated levels are common with tourniquet use, haemolysis, or delayed laboratory transit.

stem:
A 31-year-old G2P1 woman has just delivered a 4.1 kg baby vaginally at term in a tertiary obstetric unit after spontaneous labour. The third stage was actively managed with intramuscular oxytocin 10 units at delivery of the anterior shoulder. Twenty minutes after delivery she has lost an estimated 900 mL of blood and continues to bleed.

The uterus feels soft on bimanual examination and rises above the umbilicus despite massage. The placenta delivered complete and the perineum has been inspected with no significant tear. An intravenous oxytocin infusion has just been started at 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's at 250 mL/hour. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV has been administered. The maternal team is preparing the next uterotonic at the bedside. Her booking blood pressure was 118/74 and her booking medical history is unremarkable.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 108/min, BP 124/82 mmHg, RR 20/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 37.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Boggy uterus above umbilicus, perineum intact, no retained products on examination; cannulae 2 x 16G antecubital fossa, blood group and antibody screen sent
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin (pre-delivery) 120 g/L, platelets 220 x10^9/L, INR 1.0, fibrinogen 3.8 g/L (2.5-5.0); urine output measured by indwelling catheter; cross-match 4 units

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological uterotonic to give now?

options.text: Intravenous oxytocin 5 unit slow bolus repeated up to a total of 20 units in addition to the running oxytocin infusion already in progress at the bedside
options.rationale: An additional oxytocin bolus on top of an infusion does not add efficacy and may cause hypotension and arrhythmia; escalation is to a different-class uterotonic, not more oxytocin. (Source: RANZCOG Statement Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage

options.text: Intramuscular carboprost 250 micrograms repeated every 15 minutes up to a maximum total dose of 2 mg before any other uterotonic is given to this patient
options.rationale: Carboprost is reserved for ergometrine failure or contraindication; jumping straight to carboprost skips a step in the RANZCOG escalation ladder and carries bronchospasm risk. (Source: RANZCOG Statement Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage

options.text: Intramuscular ergometrine 250 micrograms repeated at 15 minute intervals up to a maximum of 1 mg in 24 hours alongside the oxytocin infusion
options.rationale: After oxytocin infusion and TXA, ergometrine 250 mcg IM is the next uterotonic step in PPH with uterine atony when there is no hypertensive or cardiac contraindication. (Source: RANZCOG Statement Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage; Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage

options.text: Rectal misoprostol 800 micrograms by single dose and reassess uterine tone and ongoing blood loss again within the next 15 minutes after administration
options.rationale: Misoprostol is a useful uterotonic where injectables are unavailable; in a tertiary unit with IV access and IM ergometrine available, ergometrine is preferred and has a faster onset. (Source: RANZCOG Statement Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage

options.text: Intramyometrial carbetocin 100 micrograms single dose injected directly through the abdominal wall under ultrasound guidance by the obstetric registrar urgently
options.rationale: Carbetocin is a long-acting oxytocin analogue used as a single IM/IV dose at delivery for prophylaxis, not as escalation after a haemorrhage has already developed despite oxytocin infusion. (Source: RANZCOG Statement Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage

explanation.summary: RANZCOG escalation for atonic PPH is sequential: bimanual compression and massage; oxytocin 10 units IM or 5 unit IV bolus then 40 unit infusion; TXA 1 g IV (within 3 hours of bleeding onset); ergometrine 250 mcg IM (contraindicated in hypertension, cardiac disease, severe pre-eclampsia); carboprost 250 mcg IM (contraindicated in asthma); misoprostol 800 mcg rectal as adjunct. Mechanical and surgical measures (intrauterine balloon tamponade, B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, hysterectomy) follow if pharmacological steps fail.
key_points: Oxytocin first, then TXA, then ergometrine, then carboprost
Ergometrine contraindicated in hypertension, ischaemic heart disease, severe pre-eclampsia
Carboprost contraindicated in asthma due to bronchospasm risk
TXA effective when given within 3 hours of bleeding onset
Carbetocin is a prophylactic, not a rescue, uterotonic
explanation.pearls: Ergometrine 250 mcg IM is the standard second-line uterotonic after oxytocin failure; check BP and rule out severe pre-eclampsia before giving because hypertensive crisis is the main adverse effect.

sources.label: RANZCOG Statement Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage

stem:
A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 27 weeks gestation attends a midwife-led shared-care clinic for a routine antenatal review. She has no significant past medical history and a BMI of 27 kg/m2. There is no family history of diabetes. The pregnancy has otherwise been uncomplicated and the dating ultrasound at 12 weeks was concordant with menstrual dates.

A 75 g oral glucose tolerance test was performed two days ago in the morning after an overnight fast. Foetal growth on most recent ultrasound is on the 50th centile, liquor volume normal, dopplers normal. Maternal blood pressure has been stable at booking values throughout. Symphysio-fundal height tracks with gestation. There is no proteinuria or glycosuria on dipstick today.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78/min, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, gravid uterus consistent with dates, foetal heart rate 142/min by Doppler, no oedema, no proteinuria on dipstick
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT (ADIPS): fasting 5.2 mmol/L (less than 5.1), 1 hour 9.8 mmol/L (less than 10.0), 2 hour 7.2 mmol/L (less than 8.5); HbA1c not performed; FBC and serum ferritin within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat the 75 g oral glucose tolerance test in 4 weeks time to confirm a true diagnosis before any management changes are made to the pregnancy plan
options.rationale: Routine OGTT repeat is not required in pregnancy when a single ADIPS value is breached; the fasting value of 5.2 mmol/L exceeds the threshold and confirms GDM. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for the Testing and Diagnosis of Hyperglycaemia in Pregnancy)

options.text: Send a confirmatory HbA1c level to differentiate undiagnosed pre-existing type 2 diabetes from new-onset gestational diabetes mellitus before any further intervention
options.rationale: HbA1c is recommended at booking to identify pre-existing diabetes, not as a confirmatory test after a 27 week OGTT; pre-existing diabetes is unlikely with these moderate values. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines)

options.text: Reassure the patient that the OGTT is normal because the 1 hour and 2 hour values are well below the upper limit of the gestational diabetes diagnostic ranges
options.rationale: ADIPS criteria require all three values to be below the cut-offs; a single breached value confirms GDM. The fasting of 5.2 mmol/L exceeds the 5.1 mmol/L threshold. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines)

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg twice daily empirically as first-line pharmacotherapy without trial of diet and exercise modification first and arrange MDT follow up
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is escalated to only after a 1-2 week trial of dietary and lifestyle intervention with home blood glucose self-monitoring fails to meet targets; not empirical at diagnosis. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines)

options.text: Diagnose gestational diabetes and arrange referral to the multidisciplinary diabetes-in-pregnancy team for dietary education and blood glucose self-monitoring
options.rationale: ADIPS diagnoses GDM with ANY ONE value at or above fasting 5.1, 1 hour 10.0, or 2 hour 8.5 mmol/L; a fasting of 5.2 mmol/L is diagnostic and triggers MDT referral. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for the Testing and Diagnosis of Hyperglycaemia in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: ADIPS diagnoses gestational diabetes from a single 75 g OGTT at 24-28 weeks gestation with any one of: fasting plasma glucose 5.1 mmol/L or higher, 1 hour 10.0 mmol/L or higher, or 2 hour 8.5 mmol/L or higher. A single breached value is diagnostic. Management starts with diet and exercise modification with capillary glucose self-monitoring (targets: fasting under 5.1, 1 hour postprandial under 7.4, 2 hour postprandial under 6.7 mmol/L). Insulin or metformin is added if targets are missed despite lifestyle adherence over 1-2 weeks. Postnatal follow-up OGTT at 6-12 weeks reclassifies persistent dysglycaemia.
key_points: ADIPS thresholds: fasting 5.1, 1 hour 10.0, 2 hour 8.5 mmol/L
Any single breached value confirms GDM
First-line management is dietary and lifestyle modification with SMBG
Insulin or metformin added after 1-2 weeks of failed lifestyle targets
Postnatal OGTT at 6-12 weeks reclassifies persistent dysglycaemia
explanation.pearls: ADIPS GDM is diagnosed by ANY ONE of the three OGTT values being met; the fasting cut-off of 5.1 mmol/L is the most commonly breached and missed, particularly when the 1 and 2 hour values look reassuring.

sources.label: RANZCOG Diagnosis of Gestational Diabetes Mellitus

stem:
A 27-year-old G2P0 woman with blood group O Rhesus D negative presents to a metropolitan early pregnancy assessment clinic at 11 weeks gestation. She reports a single small painless bright red vaginal bleed yesterday with no associated pain. She had no previous antenatal anti-D administration in this pregnancy. Her first pregnancy ended in a 6 week miscarriage.

The abdomen is soft and non-tender on examination. A transvaginal ultrasound performed today shows a single live intrauterine pregnancy with a crown rump length consistent with 11 weeks and a 1 cm subchorionic haematoma. Foetal heart rate is 162 per minute. There is no cervical bleeding or open os on speculum examination. She has not started any anti-D yet in this pregnancy. Her antibody screen done at booking was negative.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78/min, BP 110/68 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, no cervical motion tenderness, closed os on speculum; small subchorionic haematoma seen on TVUS
data_table.Investigations: Blood group O Rhesus D negative, antibody screen negative; haemoglobin 122 g/L; transvaginal ultrasound 11 week live intrauterine pregnancy with 1 cm subchorionic haematoma; quantitative bhCG not required

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding Rhesus D immunoprophylaxis?

options.text: Send foetal cell-free DNA Rhesus genotyping on a maternal sample and only administer anti-D once a Rhesus D positive foetus has been definitively confirmed
options.rationale: cffDNA RhD genotyping is available in some AU centres for routine 28 week prophylaxis but not for a sensitising event requiring action within 72 hours; treat empirically. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Administer anti-D immunoglobulin 625 IU intramuscularly within 72 hours of the bleed and document the dose on her antenatal record now
options.rationale: 625 IU is the dose for sensitising events at or beyond 12 weeks gestation (per NBA); at 11 weeks gestation the dose is 250 IU. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Hold anti-D immunoglobulin and reassess only at the next routine 28 week antenatal anti-D prophylaxis appointment if no further bleeding has occurred since today
options.rationale: A vaginal bleed at 11 weeks is a sensitising event regardless of subsequent bleeding; deferring anti-D risks alloimmunisation that cannot be reversed once antibodies are formed. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Send a maternal Kleihauer-Betke test to determine the size of any feto-maternal haemorrhage and dose the anti-D based on the calculated feto-maternal bleed
options.rationale: Kleihauer testing is required for sensitising events at or beyond 20 weeks to size larger feto-maternal haemorrhage; before 20 weeks the standard dose covers any plausible bleed. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Administer anti-D immunoglobulin 250 IU intramuscularly within 72 hours of the bleed and document the dose on her antenatal record now
options.rationale: Sensitising events before 12 weeks gestation (vaginal bleeding, ectopic, miscarriage) receive 250 IU anti-D IM within 72 hours; the Australian dose for first-trimester events is 250 IU. (Source: NBA Guidelines for the Prophylactic Use of Rh D Immunoglobulin (Anti-D) in Obstetrics)

explanation.summary: NBA Australia anti-D dosing depends on gestation: under 12 weeks 250 IU IM, 12 to under 20 weeks 250 IU IM, 20 weeks and beyond 625 IU IM plus Kleihauer-Betke quantification to detect bleeds greater than 6 mL foetal blood. Routine antenatal prophylaxis is 625 IU at 28 and 34 weeks (or single 1500 IU at 28-30 weeks per local policy). Post-delivery 625 IU within 72 hours if cord blood confirms RhD positive. Foetal RhD genotyping by cffDNA can avoid unnecessary routine prophylaxis but is not used for acute sensitising events.
key_points: Under 20 weeks sensitising event: 250 IU anti-D IM within 72 hours
20 weeks and over: 625 IU plus Kleihauer to size the bleed
Sensitising events include APH, abdominal trauma, ECV, amniocentesis, CVS, miscarriage, TOP, ectopic
Routine antenatal prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks (or 1500 IU at 28-30)
Foetal cffDNA RhD genotyping not used in acute sensitising events
explanation.pearls: Anti-D 250 IU IM is the dose for sensitising events under 20 weeks gestation in Australia, given within 72 hours; the dose steps up to 625 IU plus Kleihauer-Betke from 20 weeks onwards because feto-maternal haemorrhage volume can be larger.

sources.label: RANZCOG Statement Use of Rh D Immunoglobulin

stem:
A 33-year-old G3P1 woman at 33+2 weeks gestation is brought to a tertiary maternity unit by her partner with severe occipital headache and visual scotomas that started 2 hours ago. She has been managed in pregnancy on oral labetalol 200 mg three times daily for chronic hypertension. The booking BP was 138/88 mmHg. Her renal function and liver enzymes were normal at last review one week ago.

On arrival her BP is markedly elevated on two readings five minutes apart on the same arm. She has 3+ proteinuria. The foetus is in cephalic presentation with reassuring CTG. Magnesium sulfate loading has just been started for seizure prophylaxis. Her last labetalol dose was 6 hours ago. She has no known asthma but does have an autoimmune Raynaud phenomenon documented in her past medical history. The obstetric team has called for urgent intravenous antihypertensive therapy to be drawn up.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96/min, BP 172/118 mmHg (repeated 168/116), RR 18/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 °C; foetal HR 140/min reassuring CTG
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes with 2 beats of clonus, no facial oedema, no right upper quadrant tenderness; visual fields intact to confrontation
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis 3+ protein, urine protein:creatinine ratio 320 mg/mmol (less than 30); platelets 142 x10^9/L, ALT 28 U/L (<35), creatinine 72 micromol/L; uric acid 0.42 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate single best intravenous antihypertensive choice now?

options.text: Intravenous glyceryl trinitrate infusion at 5 micrograms per minute titrated to a target maternal mean arterial pressure of 100 mmHg in the resuscitation bay
options.rationale: GTN is reserved for pre-eclampsia with concurrent pulmonary oedema or angina; standard severe hypertension management is hydralazine, labetalol, or nifedipine immediate-release. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous labetalol 20 mg bolus repeated and doubled every 10 minutes up to a maximum of 220 mg titrated against the maternal blood pressure response
options.rationale: Beta-blockers including labetalol are best avoided in vasospastic disease such as Raynaud phenomenon because they can precipitate digital ischaemia; hydralazine is a safer first choice here. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg immediate-release capsule and reassess the maternal blood pressure response after 30 minutes from administration in the resuscitation bay
options.rationale: Sublingual or immediate-release nifedipine is associated with abrupt and uncontrolled BP drop and is not recommended in severe pre-eclampsia; oral nifedipine immediate-release tablets are the alternative if available. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous hydralazine 5 mg slow injection repeated every 20 minutes up to a maximum of 20 mg titrated against the maternal blood pressure response
options.rationale: Hydralazine is the AU first-line IV agent when oral labetalol has failed and labetalol IV is relatively contraindicated (Raynaud phenomenon is a beta-blockade caution); hydralazine acts within 20 minutes. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline; RANZCOG)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate further 2 g bolus on top of the seizure prophylaxis loading dose targeted at lowering the maternal blood pressure to safe values
options.rationale: Magnesium is for seizure prophylaxis (eclampsia), not BP control; loading is 4 g over 20 min then 1 g/h infusion. It does not substantially lower BP and additional bolus risks toxicity. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

explanation.summary: SOMANZ defines severe hypertension in pregnancy as systolic 160 mmHg or above OR diastolic 110 mmHg or above on two readings; IV antihypertensives are required within 30 minutes. First-line options are IV labetalol, IV hydralazine, or oral nifedipine immediate release - choice is patient-tailored. Labetalol is avoided in vasospastic disease (Raynaud), asthma, and bradycardia. Hydralazine carries a small reflex tachycardia and maternal-foetal hypotension risk; pre-loading with 250 mL fluid before each bolus is suggested. Magnesium sulfate is given concurrently for seizure prophylaxis but does not control BP.
key_points: Severe hypertension threshold: 160/110 mmHg on 2 readings
First-line IV options: labetalol, hydralazine, or oral nifedipine IR
Labetalol avoided in Raynaud, asthma, bradycardia
Magnesium sulfate is seizure prophylaxis, NOT BP control
GTN reserved for concurrent pulmonary oedema or angina
explanation.pearls: Hydralazine 5 mg IV every 20 minutes up to 20 mg is the SOMANZ-recommended IV antihypertensive choice when labetalol is contraindicated or has failed; pre-load with 250 mL fluid to reduce reflex maternal hypotension.

stem:
A 34-year-old G3P2 woman has been pushing for 50 minutes at full dilation in a tertiary delivery suite. Her two previous deliveries were uncomplicated vaginal births of 3.6 kg and 3.8 kg infants. Antenatal estimated foetal weight by growth scan one week ago was 4.4 kg; her booking BMI was 32 kg/m2. She has a diet-controlled gestational diabetes diagnosis from this pregnancy.

The foetal head delivers with classical turtling against the perineum. With gentle downward axial traction by the registrar the anterior shoulder will not deliver. The emergency buzzer has been pressed and the obstetric consultant, anaesthetist, neonatal team, and senior midwife are arriving. The clock has just started. The mother is positioned semi-recumbent at the edge of the bed. She is haemodynamically stable. The midwife is asking what the first manoeuvre should be.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 102/min, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 37.2 °C; foetal HR 138/min by Doppler immediately after head delivery
data_table.Examination: Foetal head delivered with turtling against perineum, no spontaneous restitution; anterior shoulder impacted behind maternal pubic symphysis on gentle axial traction
data_table.Investigations: Cardiotocograph was reassuring in second stage; no other investigations pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate single best initial manoeuvre to attempt?

options.text: McRoberts manoeuvre with hyperflexion of the maternal hips against the abdomen by two assistants standing on either side of the delivery bed
options.rationale: McRoberts is the first-line manoeuvre in shoulder dystocia per HELPERR (Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs in McRoberts, Pressure suprapubic, Enter rotational manoeuvres, Remove posterior arm, Roll); resolves over 40% of cases. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia; PROMPT Course)

options.text: Apply suprapubic pressure aimed in the downward and lateral direction to dislodge the impacted anterior shoulder from behind the maternal pubic symphysis
options.rationale: Suprapubic pressure is paired with McRoberts as the second step of HELPERR; the standard sequence runs McRoberts first, then add suprapubic pressure if McRoberts alone fails. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia; PROMPT Course)

options.text: Generous mediolateral episiotomy under local anaesthetic infiltration to enlarge the soft tissue space and allow direct access for the planned manoeuvres
options.rationale: Shoulder dystocia is a bony, not soft tissue, problem; episiotomy does not resolve the dystocia but may be performed selectively to enable internal manoeuvres later. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Direct manual removal of the posterior arm of the foetus by sweeping it across the chest before any external manoeuvres have been attempted at this delivery
options.rationale: Posterior arm delivery is part of HELPERR but follows McRoberts and suprapubic pressure; it is a third-line internal manoeuvre, not a first-line approach. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Apply firm downward fundal pressure by an assistant standing behind the maternal head to push the impacted anterior foetal shoulder downwards and forwards
options.rationale: Fundal pressure increases impaction and risks uterine rupture; it is contraindicated in shoulder dystocia. Suprapubic pressure (not fundal) is the correct external adjunct. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is an unpredictable obstetric emergency requiring a sequenced response; the HELPERR mnemonic (Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs in McRoberts, Pressure suprapubic, Enter rotational manoeuvres, Remove posterior arm, Roll the patient) provides a structured approach. McRoberts alone resolves over 40% of cases; combined McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure resolves over 80%. Internal manoeuvres (Rubin II, Woods screw, reverse Woods, posterior arm delivery) follow. Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated because it worsens impaction and risks uterine rupture and brachial plexus injury. Time-call documentation (head-to-body interval) and senior staff escalation begin immediately.
key_points: HELPERR is the standard structured sequence
McRoberts is always the first manoeuvre
Suprapubic pressure (NEVER fundal) is the next step
Internal manoeuvres only after external have failed
Time-call documentation begins from head delivery
explanation.pearls: McRoberts manoeuvre alone resolves over 40% of shoulder dystocia cases; pair with suprapubic pressure to lift resolution above 80% before progressing to internal manoeuvres.

subtopic_detail: Ovarian torsion diagnosis in normal Doppler
stem:
A 19-year-old nulliparous woman presents to a metropolitan emergency department with 8 hours of sudden onset severe right iliac fossa pain that began while she was at the gym doing burpees. She has vomited 4 times. The pain is constant with intermittent spikes. She is normally cycling, with her last menstrual period 12 days ago, and is sexually active using condoms.

On examination she is in significant pain at rest, lying still. The right iliac fossa is tender with voluntary guarding but no rebound. Her right adnexa is exquisitely tender on bimanual examination. Urine bhCG is negative. A transvaginal ultrasound reports a right ovary of 6.2 cm volume with a 3 cm haemorrhagic-appearing cyst, no free fluid in the pelvis, and preserved arterial and venous Doppler flow signals from the right ovarian vessels. The left ovary is normal at 4.1 cm volume. Pain control with oral oxycodone has not relieved her significantly.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 108/min, BP 122/74 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 37.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Right iliac fossa tenderness with voluntary guarding, exquisite right adnexal tenderness on bimanual; no peritonism, no cervical motion tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Urine bhCG negative; haemoglobin 130 g/L, WCC 12.4 x10^9/L, CRP 8 mg/L (<5); transvaginal ultrasound right ovary 6.2 cm volume with 3 cm haemorrhagic cyst, preserved arterial and venous Doppler signals; urine microscopy bland

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge home with oral analgesia and arrange outpatient gynaecology review in 1 week given that ultrasound Doppler signals from the right ovary are preserved
options.rationale: Doppler arterial and venous flow can be preserved in early or intermittent ovarian torsion (sensitivity around 50%); clinical findings of acute adnexal pain plus an enlarged ovary outweigh a reassuring Doppler. (Source: RANZCOG Position Statement: Ovarian Torsion; UpToDate)

options.text: Trial of intravenous opiate analgesia and antispasmodic medication and reassessment of clinical signs and pain scores after 2 hours of adequate analgesia first
options.rationale: Adequate analgesia is appropriate alongside surgical preparation but is not the definitive step; pain resolution may be misleading because the ovary can be necrosing while pain settles. (Source: RANZCOG Position Statement: Ovarian Torsion)

options.text: Repeat the transvaginal ultrasound with Doppler in 4 to 6 hours to look for any interval change in arterial or venous flow signals or any size increase
options.rationale: Watchful waiting for 4 to 6 hours risks irreversible ischaemia in a torsion; the diagnosis is surgical and must not be delayed by serial imaging. (Source: RANZCOG Position Statement: Ovarian Torsion)

options.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the pelvis with contrast to clarify the ovarian appearances and viability before a final surgical decision is made by gynaecology
options.rationale: MRI is rarely indicated in acute torsion; it is time-consuming, often unavailable out of hours, and does not change the surgical pathway. (Source: RANZCOG Position Statement: Ovarian Torsion)

options.text: Urgent diagnostic laparoscopy by the gynaecology team aiming for ovarian detorsion and conservation regardless of macroscopic appearance at first inspection
options.rationale: Suspected ovarian torsion is a surgical diagnosis; preserved Doppler does not exclude it. Laparoscopy with detorsion and conservative management preserves fertility in most cases, even when the ovary looks ischaemic. (Source: RANZCOG Position Statement: Ovarian Torsion)

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion is a clinical and surgical diagnosis; imaging is supportive but not exclusionary. Risk factors include ovarian enlargement above 5 cm volume, mass lesions (dermoid, functional cyst), pregnancy, ovulation induction, and sudden exertion. Doppler ultrasound has a sensitivity of only around 50% because the ovary has dual blood supply via ovarian and uterine arteries; preserved flow does NOT rule out torsion. The treatment of choice is urgent laparoscopic detorsion with conservation of the ovary regardless of macroscopic appearance at first inspection; up to 90% of black or purple ovaries recover after untwisting. Oophorectomy is reserved for clearly necrotic ovaries or post-menopausal patients.
key_points: Ovarian torsion is a clinical and surgical diagnosis
Doppler ultrasound sensitivity is only 50%
Conservative detorsion preserves the ovary in most cases
Black appearance at laparoscopy still warrants untwisting
Oophorectomy reserved for clear necrosis or post-menopausal patient
explanation.pearls: Preserved ovarian Doppler does NOT rule out torsion; the ovary has dual blood supply via ovarian and uterine arteries, and clinical suspicion plus an enlarged tender adnexa is enough to take a young woman to laparoscopy.

stem:
A 36-year-old G4P3 woman is reviewed in a tertiary maternal-foetal medicine clinic at 30 weeks gestation. She has had three previous caesarean sections without complication and is now booked for a fourth planned caesarean. The current pregnancy is uncomplicated. Foetal growth is on the 50th centile. Her booking BMI is 28 kg/m2 and she is otherwise well.

A detailed ultrasound at 28 weeks describes an anterior placenta covering the internal cervical os. The report flags loss of the retroplacental clear zone, two large placental lacunae with turbulent flow, and bridging vessels between the placenta and the bladder. A confirmatory MRI shows myometrial thinning to 1 mm between the placenta and the bladder, focal bulging of the placenta into the bladder serosa, but no clear breach of the bladder wall itself. She has been counselled that planned delivery will be early.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 80/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.6 °C; foetal HR 142/min reassuring
data_table.Examination: Gravid uterus consistent with 30 weeks; no abdominal tenderness; symphysio-fundal height 30 cm; no vaginal bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound and MRI consistent with placenta percreta involving bladder serosa; haemoglobin 118 g/L, ferritin 24 mcg/L (15-200), platelets 232 x10^9/L, group and antibody screen negative; cell saver and urology available at the tertiary unit

lead_in: What is the most appropriate planned mode and timing of delivery to recommend at the multidisciplinary planning meeting?

options.text: Spontaneous vaginal delivery at term with continuous CTG monitoring and active management of the third stage including immediate manual removal of placenta
options.rationale: Vaginal delivery is contraindicated in placenta percreta with placenta praevia; manual removal causes massive haemorrhage and bladder injury. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Placenta Praevia and Accreta Spectrum; FIGO)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement: Placenta Praevia and Accreta Spectrum
FIGO consensus guidelines on placenta accreta spectrum disorders

options.text: Planned caesarean hysterectomy at 34 to 36 weeks gestation at a tertiary centre with multidisciplinary team, cell saver, urology back-up, and antenatal corticosteroids
options.rationale: RANZCOG and FIGO recommend planned caesarean hysterectomy at 34-36+0 weeks for placenta accreta spectrum with MDT support; trying to remove the placenta or going to term is associated with catastrophic haemorrhage. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Placenta Praevia and Accreta Spectrum; FIGO)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement: Placenta Praevia and Accreta Spectrum
FIGO consensus guidelines on placenta accreta spectrum disorders

options.text: Planned caesarean section at 39 weeks gestation with attempted manual placental removal followed by uterotonics and balloon tamponade if heavy bleeding ensues
options.rationale: Term timing increases risk of unscheduled bleeding labour; attempted manual removal in placenta accreta spectrum is associated with massive haemorrhage and exsanguination. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Placenta Praevia and Accreta Spectrum)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement: Placenta Praevia and Accreta Spectrum

options.text: Planned caesarean section at 37 weeks gestation with placenta left in situ and outpatient methotrexate therapy aiming for delayed resorption over 6 months postpartum
options.rationale: Leaving the placenta in situ is an option in selected cases (fertility preserving) but is associated with sepsis, secondary haemorrhage, and DIC; not first-line in placenta percreta with bladder involvement. Methotrexate adds no proven benefit. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Placenta Praevia and Accreta Spectrum)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement: Placenta Praevia and Accreta Spectrum

options.text: Planned classical caesarean section at 32 weeks gestation followed by uterine artery embolisation in the angiography suite as planned definitive haemorrhage control
options.rationale: 32 weeks is too early without specific indication and exposes the neonate to avoidable prematurity morbidity; UAE is an adjunct, not a substitute for caesarean hysterectomy in placenta percreta with bladder involvement. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Placenta Praevia and Accreta Spectrum)
source_refs: RANZCOG Statement: Placenta Praevia and Accreta Spectrum

explanation.summary: Placenta accreta spectrum (accreta, increta, percreta) is among the leading causes of maternal mortality due to massive haemorrhage. Major risk factors are prior caesarean section count (cumulative) plus placenta praevia. Antenatal ultrasound features include loss of the retroplacental clear zone, placental lacunae, abnormal vasculature, and bladder bulging; MRI defines bladder and parametrial involvement. Planned caesarean hysterectomy at 34-36+0 weeks gestation in a tertiary centre with cell saver, MDT, urology, interventional radiology, and ICU on standby is the standard of care; antenatal corticosteroids are given for foetal lung maturity. Conservative management (placenta left in situ) has a role in fertility preservation but is associated with significant late morbidity.
key_points: Planned delivery at 34-36+0 weeks at tertiary centre
Caesarean hysterectomy is standard of care for percreta with bladder involvement
MDT: obstetric, anaesthetic, urology, interventional radiology, blood bank, ICU
Antenatal corticosteroids before planned delivery for foetal lung maturity
Conservative management is fertility-preserving but with significant late morbidity
explanation.pearls: Placenta accreta spectrum delivery is planned at 34-36+0 weeks in a tertiary centre with cell saver and MDT support; never attempt manual placental removal because catastrophic haemorrhage is the rule.

sources.label: RANZCOG Statement: Placenta Praevia and Accreta Spectrum
FIGO consensus guidelines on placenta accreta spectrum disorders

stem:
A 54-year-old man with a 20-year history of bipolar I disorder is brought to a metropolitan emergency department by his wife. He has been increasingly tremulous and unsteady on his feet over the past 4 days. He vomited twice this morning before presentation. He is normally maintained on lithium carbonate 800 mg nightly, with the last serum lithium level at 0.7 mmol/L two months ago. His wife notes that he began ramipril 5 mg daily 3 weeks ago for newly diagnosed hypertension by his GP.

On examination he is awake but drowsy and has coarse hand tremor and ataxia on standing. He is oriented to person but not place or date. Speech is dysarthric. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk with sustained ankle clonus. The abdomen is soft, mucous membranes dry. His regular lithium dose was taken this morning at home. A bedside ECG shows sinus rhythm with QTc 460 ms; no T wave changes. Vital signs are stable. The toxicology service has been contacted.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96/min, BP 138/86 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, coarse hand tremor, dysarthria, brisk reflexes with sustained ankle clonus, ataxia on standing; mucous membranes dry; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Serum lithium 2.4 mmol/L (0.4-1.0), sodium 144 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, urea 12.4 mmol/L (2.5-7.5), creatinine 165 micromol/L (baseline 78); TSH within normal limits; bicarbonate 22 mmol/L; ECG sinus rhythm QTc 460 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Hold the next dose of lithium and recheck serum lithium level in 12 hours while continuing oral fluids and rest at home with the wife monitoring symptoms
options.rationale: Outpatient management is unsafe with neurological toxicity, lithium of 2.4 mmol/L, and AKI; severe lithium toxicity requires admission and IV rehydration with consideration of dialysis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Stop lithium and ramipril, give intravenous 0.9% saline at 1.5 to 2 times maintenance and refer urgently to nephrology for consideration of haemodialysis
options.rationale: Lithium 2.4 mmol/L with neurological toxicity (drowsiness, ataxia, dysarthria, clonus) and AKI meets EXTRIP criteria for haemodialysis; saline and stopping lithium and the precipitant (ACE inhibitor) are essential immediate steps. (Source: EXTRIP Lithium Workgroup Recommendations; Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 50 g orally now as a single dose to reduce lithium absorption and to limit further increases in serum lithium concentration
options.rationale: Activated charcoal does NOT bind lithium and has no role in lithium toxicity; whole bowel irrigation has a role in massive ingestion of sustained-release lithium only. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg now and aim to alkalinise the urine to a pH greater than 7.5 to increase the renal elimination of lithium
options.rationale: Urinary alkalinisation does not enhance lithium elimination; saline rehydration and dialysis are the mainstays. Bicarbonate is used for tricyclic antidepressant and salicylate toxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Substitute oral lithium with intravenous sodium valproate loading dose 20 mg/kg to maintain mood stabilisation throughout the management of his lithium toxicity
options.rationale: Acute toxicity is a separate problem from maintenance bipolar therapy; switching to valproate during the toxicity admission is not the priority and is decided by psychiatry after recovery. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Lithium toxicity is graded by neurological features rather than serum level alone because tissue distribution lags serum. Mild toxicity (1.5-2.0 mmol/L) gives tremor and GI symptoms; moderate (2.0-2.5) adds dysarthria, ataxia, clonus, drowsiness; severe (over 2.5 or any level with seizures, coma, severe symptoms) requires dialysis. Precipitants include ACE inhibitors, ARBs, thiazides, NSAIDs, dehydration, and renal impairment. EXTRIP recommends haemodialysis for severe neurological toxicity, level above 4.0 mmol/L (or 2.5 with renal impairment), seizures, or coma. Intermittent haemodialysis is preferred over CRRT for faster lithium removal.
key_points: Tissue lithium lags serum level; neurology guides severity
Precipitants: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, thiazides, NSAIDs, dehydration, AKI
Charcoal does not bind lithium; whole bowel irrigation only for massive SR ingestion
EXTRIP threshold for dialysis: severe neurology, level above 4.0 (or 2.5 with AKI), seizures, coma
Stop lithium, IV saline, stop precipitant, dialysis
explanation.pearls: Lithium level alone does not classify severity; coarse tremor, ataxia, dysarthria, drowsiness, and clonus in a chronic user trigger EXTRIP dialysis criteria even at lower levels because tissue lithium lags serum.

stem:
A 24-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been on clozapine for 18 days, currently titrated to 200 mg nightly. He attends his community mental health clinic for routine review and reports 4 days of intermittent fevers up to 38.4 °C, fatigue, and one episode of palpitations the night before. He denies cough, dysuria, or rash. His neutrophil count remains within range on weekly FBC monitoring.

On examination he appears tired but alert. He has resting tachycardia at 116/min and warm peripheries. Heart sounds are dual, no murmurs are audible. Chest is clear. There is no calf tenderness or rash. The clinic nurse has obtained the standard week-2 clozapine screening bloods earlier today. He is otherwise compliant with his medication and lives with his mother who reports no concerning behaviours. He has had no recent overseas travel.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 116/min, BP 124/74 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 38.2 °C
data_table.Examination: Tachycardic regular rhythm, no murmurs, no pericardial rub, no lower limb oedema; clear chest; no rash; warm peripheries
data_table.Investigations: Neutrophils 3.6 x10^9/L (1.5-7.5); CRP 86 mg/L (<5); troponin I 65 ng/L (<14); ALT within normal limits; bedside ECG sinus tachycardia, no ST changes, no T wave inversion; viral swab not yet sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Withhold the next clozapine dose, refer urgently to the emergency department for transthoracic echocardiography and same-day cardiology review including repeat troponin
options.rationale: Onset of fever, tachycardia, and elevated troponin and CRP within 4 weeks of clozapine initiation is the classical clozapine-induced myocarditis triad; the AU clozapine screening protocol mandates urgent ED referral with TTE and cardiology.
