(Source: RANZCP Position Statement: Clozapine-induced Myocarditis; AMH)

options.text: Continue clozapine at the same dose, start oral paracetamol for the fever and recheck inflammatory markers and troponin in 5 days at the next routine clinic appointment
options.rationale: Outpatient observation with rising troponin and CRP during the high-risk first 4 weeks of clozapine misses myocarditis which can progress rapidly to cardiogenic shock; this needs same-day specialist assessment. (Source: RANZCP Position Statement: Clozapine-induced Myocarditis)

options.text: Switch from clozapine to olanzapine immediately at an equivalent dose and arrange a routine follow up appointment with his community psychiatrist within 2 weeks
options.rationale: Switching antipsychotics does not address the immediate concern of possible myocarditis which needs ED workup; only after exclusion of myocarditis can clozapine be ceased and an alternative discussed. (Source: RANZCP Position Statement: Clozapine-induced Myocarditis)

options.text: Repeat the full blood count and inflammatory markers urgently and only refer to the emergency department if the absolute neutrophil count drops below 1.5 x10^9/L this morning
options.rationale: Neutropenia is a separate clozapine adverse effect; the current concern (fever plus tachycardia plus troponin rise plus CRP rise) is myocarditis, not neutropenia. ANC is currently normal. (Source: RANZCP Position Statement: Clozapine-induced Myocarditis)

options.text: Withhold clozapine and commence oral roxithromycin 300 mg daily for 7 days empirically for atypical respiratory infection given the fevers, tachycardia and elevated CRP
options.rationale: Atypical pneumonia is in the differential but troponin rise on a normal chest examination and clear chest x-ray points more strongly to myocarditis; empirical antibiotics alone are inadequate. (Source: RANZCP Position Statement: Clozapine-induced Myocarditis)

explanation.summary: Clozapine-induced myocarditis occurs in around 1% of new initiators and usually presents within the first 4 weeks of treatment. The screening protocol mandates baseline and weekly troponin, CRP, and ECG for the first 4 weeks. Any fever, tachycardia, chest pain, dyspnoea, troponin rise (any value), or CRP above 100 mg/L triggers same-day cardiology referral with echocardiography. Mortality is around 10% without intervention. Clozapine is permanently ceased once myocarditis is confirmed; alternatives include olanzapine or risperidone but neither matches clozapine's efficacy in treatment-resistant schizophrenia. Neutropenia is monitored separately with weekly FBC.
key_points: Clozapine myocarditis usually presents within the first 4 weeks
Screening: weekly troponin, CRP, ECG for 4 weeks
Trigger thresholds: fever, tachycardia, any troponin rise, CRP above 100 mg/L
Mortality 10% without intervention; cardiology and echo same-day
Clozapine permanently ceased once myocarditis confirmed
explanation.pearls: Clozapine-induced myocarditis classically presents within 4 weeks of starting; ANY troponin rise plus fever plus tachycardia is the trigger to refer to ED for echocardiography even when the chest examination is unremarkable.

subtopic_detail: SSRI choice in early pregnancy with prior remission
stem:
A 31-year-old G2P1 woman attends her GP at 7 weeks gestation. She has been in remission from a moderate major depressive episode for the past 18 months on paroxetine 20 mg daily. Her first child is now 3 years old; she had antenatal anxiety but no significant relapse during that previous pregnancy on the same medication. She and her partner planned this pregnancy and were aware that paroxetine has a different safety profile in pregnancy.

She is currently euthymic with stable mood, sleep, and appetite. She has no suicidal ideation. She had one mild relapse during a previous attempt to discontinue paroxetine 2 years ago. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable and she is on no other medications. Folate 5 mg daily was started by her GP at preconception counselling. The GP has asked your community perinatal mental health service for an opinion on the medication.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78/min, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Euthymic affect, full range; no psychomotor change; no psychotic features; no thoughts of self harm; abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Investigations: FBC, electrolytes, thyroid function, vitamin D within normal limits; bhCG positive (date-concordant); foetal ultrasound at 6 weeks confirmed single intrauterine pregnancy with cardiac activity

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her antidepressant management?

options.text: Cease paroxetine immediately and review her in 4 weeks for symptomatic relapse and consider non-pharmacological therapy if symptoms recur during her pregnancy
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of paroxetine carries a high risk of relapse (relapse rate 60% if antidepressants stopped in pregnancy) and risks foetal SSRI withdrawal; planned cross-taper is safer. (Source: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period National Clinical Guideline)
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period National Clinical Guideline

options.text: Refer to perinatal psychiatry for an inpatient assessment and consideration of electroconvulsive therapy because she has a relapsing major depressive episode in pregnancy
options.rationale: ECT is reserved for severe, treatment-resistant, or life-threatening perinatal depression with psychosis or imminent suicide risk; this woman is currently euthymic. (Source: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period National Clinical Guideline)
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period National Clinical Guideline

options.text: Continue paroxetine 20 mg daily as her current effective treatment given the risks of relapse outweigh any small concerns regarding paroxetine in pregnancy outcomes
options.rationale: Paroxetine has a small but real association with first-trimester cardiac defects (especially ventricular septal defects); switching to sertraline is preferable when remission has been stable and a safer alternative exists. (Source: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period National Clinical Guideline)
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period National Clinical Guideline

options.text: Switch paroxetine to mirtazapine 15 mg nightly with reassessment in 4 weeks given mirtazapine has a better established safety profile in pregnancy than the SSRI class
options.rationale: Mirtazapine is not first-line in pregnancy and the safety database is smaller than for SSRIs; sertraline has the most pregnancy and lactation evidence in the AU setting. (Source: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period National Clinical Guideline)
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period National Clinical Guideline

options.text: Cross-taper from paroxetine to sertraline 50 mg daily over 2 weeks and continue throughout pregnancy and lactation given her history of relapse on discontinuation
options.rationale: Sertraline is the preferred SSRI in pregnancy due to low transplacental and breast milk levels; cross-tapering avoids relapse and the small increased risk of cardiac defects with first-trimester paroxetine. (Source: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period National Clinical Guideline)
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period National Clinical Guideline

explanation.summary: COPE guidelines recommend continuing antidepressants in pregnancy when there has been a prior moderate-severe episode and remission depends on medication; relapse risk on cessation is around 60%. Sertraline is the preferred SSRI in pregnancy and lactation because of low transplacental and breast milk transfer and the largest reassuring evidence base. Paroxetine carries a small first-trimester association with cardiac malformations (especially ventricular septal defects). Fluoxetine has the longest half-life and higher breast milk transfer. Cross-tapering from one SSRI to another over 1-2 weeks is the standard transition method. Non-pharmacological therapies (CBT, IPT) are adjuncts, not substitutes, when relapse risk is high.
key_points: Sertraline is the preferred SSRI in pregnancy and lactation in Australia
Paroxetine first-trimester carries small VSD association
Stopping antidepressants in pregnancy has 60% relapse rate
Cross-taper over 1-2 weeks is the standard transition
ECT reserved for severe perinatal depression with psychosis or imminent risk
explanation.pearls: Sertraline is the preferred SSRI in pregnancy and lactation because of low breast milk transfer (under 2% relative infant dose) and the largest reassuring safety dataset; cross-taper from paroxetine over 1-2 weeks before week 8 of gestation.

sources.label: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period National Clinical Guideline
RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Clinical Practice Guideline

stem:
A 45-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder is admitted to a general medical ward after her family found her standing in the kitchen at home, not moving and not responding for 4 hours. She had stopped eating and drinking 36 hours earlier. Her usual medications are lithium and quetiapine. Her last manic episode was 3 years ago; she has had two previous similar episodes which the family describes as periods of complete withdrawal. There is no recent fever, illness, or drug use.

On arrival she is mute, with a fixed waxy posture of her arms held above the head when placed by the examiner, which she maintains for over 5 minutes. She does not respond to verbal command but tracks the examiner with her eyes. She has rigidity without tremor. Bedside fluids and an indwelling urinary catheter have been placed. Her recent lithium level was therapeutic. Bloods are unremarkable. CT head shows no acute findings. Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale score is 16.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 102/min, BP 124/78 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 37.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Mute, waxy flexibility (catalepsy), arms maintained above head over 5 minutes; tracking with eyes; rigidity without tremor; no fever, no clonus, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, urea and creatinine within normal limits; CK 168 U/L; lithium level 0.8 mmol/L; venous blood gas pH 7.40; CT head no acute findings; bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale 16

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence intravenous dantrolene 1 mg/kg in addition to her usual psychiatric medications targeting the rigidity component present on her clinical examination today
options.rationale: NMS classically has hyperthermia, autonomic instability, marked CK rise (often above 1000), and altered consciousness; her CK is only 168 and she has no fever or autonomic instability - this points to catatonia, not NMS. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Mood Disorders

options.text: Refer urgently for unilateral right-sided electroconvulsive therapy under general anaesthesia at the next available list before any pharmacological intervention is trialled first
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective in catatonia (especially malignant or lorazepam-non-responsive) but the diagnostic lorazepam challenge precedes ECT in most protocols. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Mood Disorders

options.text: Increase her quetiapine to 600 mg daily for better antipsychotic cover and continue supportive care with intravenous fluids and nasogastric feeding for nutrition
options.rationale: Antipsychotic dose escalation in catatonia worsens it and can precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome; antipsychotics are deprescribed and benzodiazepines are first-line. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Mood Disorders

options.text: Administer lorazepam 2 mg intravenously as a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge and reassess catatonic features and Bush-Francis score within 30 to 60 minutes
options.rationale: Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV is the AU and international first-line diagnostic challenge for suspected catatonia; over 70% of patients respond within 30-60 minutes and this is both diagnostic and therapeutic. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Mood Disorders; UpToDate Catatonia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Mood Disorders

options.text: Hold lithium and quetiapine for 24 hours and reassess catatonic features tomorrow morning with the consultant psychiatry team before any further therapeutic intervention
options.rationale: Stopping lithium risks bipolar relapse and does not address the immediate catatonic state; benzodiazepine challenge plus supportive care is the priority. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Mood Disorders

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a syndrome of motor, behavioural, and autonomic disturbance occurring in primary psychiatric (mood disorders most commonly), neurological (encephalitis, NMDA receptor antibody), and medical (electrolytes, infection) contexts. Diagnostic criteria require 3 or more of 12 features (stupor, catalepsy, waxy flexibility, mutism, negativism, posturing, mannerism, stereotypy, agitation, grimacing, echolalia, echopraxia). Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV is the diagnostic and therapeutic challenge; over 70% respond within 30-60 minutes. ECT is the next-line if lorazepam fails or for malignant catatonia (hyperthermia, autonomic instability). Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and risk NMS; benzodiazepine titration up to 6-8 mg lorazepam daily is often required. The condition is reversible when recognised; missed catatonia carries high morbidity from immobility complications.
key_points: Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale is the standard severity tool
Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV is the diagnostic and therapeutic challenge
Over 70% of patients respond within 30-60 minutes
Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and risk NMS
ECT is first-line for lorazepam-non-responsive or malignant catatonia
explanation.pearls: The lorazepam challenge (1-2 mg IV) is both diagnostic and therapeutic in catatonia; over 70% improve within 30-60 minutes, and persistent benzodiazepine cover plus deprescribed antipsychotics is the standard maintenance approach.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Mood Disorders

subtopic_detail: Smoking cessation pharmacotherapy in MDD remission
stem:
A 42-year-old man attends his GP requesting help to quit smoking. He has smoked 25 cigarettes daily for 25 years (about 30 pack years). He has tried nicotine patches twice with relapse within 2 weeks. He has a history of major depressive disorder with one suicide attempt at age 28; he has been in stable remission for the past 7 years on sertraline 100 mg daily. There is no current depression.

His Heaviness of Smoking Index is 5 of 6 (smokes within 5 minutes of waking, over 30 cigarettes per day). Mood remains stable, with no recent psychosocial stressors. He has no history of seizures, alcohol dependence, or eating disorder. He has no cardiovascular disease and a recent ECG was normal. He is otherwise systemically well. His asthma is well controlled on a low-dose ICS-LABA. He has been screened with the PHQ-9 today which is 3 of 27 indicating no current depression.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76/min, BP 124/78 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 97% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mild bilateral wheeze on forced expiration; no clubbing; cardiovascular examination unremarkable; mental state examination euthymic, no suicidal ideation
data_table.Investigations: FBC, electrolytes, LFTs within normal limits; ECG sinus rhythm normal axes and intervals; eGFR over 60 mL/min/1.73m2; PHQ-9 score 3 of 27

lead_in: What is the most appropriate single best initial pharmacotherapy to recommend?

options.text: Bupropion sustained-release 150 mg daily for 3 days then 150 mg twice daily for 7 to 12 weeks with set quit day 1 week into the titration and review at 4 weeks
options.rationale: Bupropion is a reasonable alternative but reduces seizure threshold and interacts with sertraline (CYP2D6 and serotonergic effects); in a stable depression patient on SSRI, varenicline is preferred. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Combination nicotine replacement therapy with 21 mg patch daily plus a 4 mg gum or lozenge as needed and review the patient again in 2 weeks for clinical follow up
options.rationale: Combination NRT is a reasonable alternative but he has already failed monotherapy NRT twice and his heaviness of smoking suggests pharmacotherapy with the highest efficacy is justified; varenicline outperforms combination NRT. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days then 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days then 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks with set quit day 1 week into the titration
options.rationale: Varenicline is the most efficacious AU first-line pharmacotherapy for moderate-heavy dependence; the EAGLES trial showed it does NOT increase neuropsychiatric events in stable mood disorders. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation: A Guide for Health Professionals; AMH)

options.text: Nortriptyline titrated up to 75 to 100 mg nightly for 12 weeks with set quit day 1 week into the titration after careful baseline ECG and serum nortriptyline level taken
options.rationale: Nortriptyline is a second-line agent for smoking cessation, has significant anticholinergic side effects, and combines with SSRI increases serotonergic risk; varenicline is safer and more effective. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Behavioural counselling via Quitline alone without pharmacotherapy as initial approach given his prior history of major depressive disorder and concern about pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Counselling alone has lower quit rates than counselling plus pharmacotherapy; with high dependence and prior NRT failures, behavioural treatment alone is inadequate. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

explanation.summary: First-line pharmacotherapy options for smoking cessation in Australia are varenicline, combination NRT (patch plus oral), or bupropion SR. Varenicline is the most effective single agent with around 25% 1-year quit rates. The EAGLES trial demonstrated no increased neuropsychiatric events in stable psychiatric patients, including those with stable depression. Bupropion lowers seizure threshold, is contraindicated in eating disorders and recent alcohol withdrawal, and interacts with SSRIs. NRT combination (patch plus short-acting) is equivalent to varenicline in some studies and is the preferred choice in pregnancy. Counselling plus pharmacotherapy is the additive standard.
key_points: Varenicline is the most effective single AU first-line agent
EAGLES trial - varenicline is safe in stable mood disorders
Bupropion lowers seizure threshold and interacts with SSRIs
Combination NRT (patch plus oral) is equivalent to varenicline
Counselling plus pharmacotherapy beats either alone
explanation.pearls: Varenicline 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks following a 1-week titration produces around 25% 1-year quit rate in heavy smokers; EAGLES showed no neuropsychiatric signal in stable mood disorders despite older PBS warnings.

subtopic_detail: Adult ADHD pharmacotherapy choice in cocaine history
stem:
A 28-year-old man self-refers to a private psychiatry practice for assessment of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. He describes lifelong distractibility, difficulty completing tasks, hyperactivity, and emotional dysregulation. Symptoms started before age 12 and have impaired schooling, employment, and relationships. A teacher-completed Vanderbilt scale from his year 4 school report flags 7 of 9 inattentive items.

He is currently a software developer but has been performance managed twice in the last 12 months. He drinks 4 standard drinks weekly. He used cocaine recreationally for 4 years and stopped 18 months ago after a near-fatal cardiac arrhythmia event in a nightclub. He has no current substance use. He has no other psychiatric history. ECG shows sinus rhythm with QTc 410 ms and otherwise normal. Echocardiogram (recent) was normal. He is asking specifically about medication options.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72/min, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mental state examination - mood euthymic, attention impaired during interview, no psychotic features, no suicidal ideation
data_table.Investigations: FBC, electrolytes, LFTs, thyroid within normal limits; urine drug screen negative; ECG sinus rhythm QTc 410 ms; echocardiogram normal LV function; ASRS-v1.1 score 6 of 6 positive screening items

lead_in: What is the most appropriate single best initial pharmacotherapy to recommend?

options.text: Methylphenidate immediate-release 5 mg twice daily titrated weekly as first-line stimulant therapy given his clear positive screening and lifelong impairment
options.rationale: Stimulants are relatively contraindicated in patients with a history of stimulant-induced cardiac arrhythmia; non-stimulant therapy is safer first-line in this specific context. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guideline 23: Assessment and Treatment of ADHD; AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: RANZCP Practice Guideline 23: Assessment and Treatment of ADHD
AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Lisdexamfetamine 30 mg daily titrated weekly as a long-acting prodrug stimulant with smoother pharmacokinetics and lower diversion potential than other stimulants
options.rationale: Although lisdexamfetamine has a smoother profile than dexamphetamine, all stimulants share cardiac effects; with a documented arrhythmia event on cocaine, non-stimulant alternatives are first-line. (Source: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Guanfacine extended-release 1 mg daily titrated weekly to 4 mg daily as a non-stimulant alpha-2 agonist given his history of stimulant-induced cardiac arrhythmia event
options.rationale: Guanfacine XR is approved for paediatric ADHD in Australia and is not first-line in adults; atomoxetine has the strongest adult AU evidence and is the recommended non-stimulant when stimulants are contraindicated. (Source: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Bupropion sustained-release 150 mg daily for 3 days then 150 mg twice daily for ADHD as an off-label noradrenergic-dopaminergic option given his stimulant contraindication
options.rationale: Bupropion has some evidence in adult ADHD but is not first-line in Australia and lowers seizure threshold; atomoxetine is the AADPA-recommended non-stimulant. (Source: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Atomoxetine 40 mg daily titrated to 80 mg after 1 week as a non-stimulant first-line agent given his history of stimulant-induced cardiac arrhythmia and current ADHD diagnosis
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is the AU first-line non-stimulant for ADHD; AADPA recommends it as the first-line drug when stimulants are contraindicated, including patients with prior stimulant-induced arrhythmia. (Source: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD; RANZCP Practice Guideline 23)
source_refs: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD
RANZCP Practice Guideline 23: Assessment and Treatment of ADHD

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD diagnosis requires DSM-5-TR criteria with onset before age 12, persistence into adulthood, impairment in 2 or more settings, and exclusion of other mental health conditions causing the symptoms. AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline recommends stimulants (methylphenidate or lisdexamfetamine) as first-line for adults without contraindications. Cardiac contraindications include structural heart disease, prior stimulant-induced arrhythmia, uncontrolled hypertension, and concurrent stimulant misuse. Atomoxetine is the first-line non-stimulant. Driving advice and S8 prescribing rules (state-specific authority required) apply. Baseline ECG, BP, HR, and weight are mandatory; follow-up every 3 months in the first year.
key_points: AADPA guidance: stimulants first-line in adults without contraindication
Prior stimulant-induced arrhythmia is a relative contraindication
Atomoxetine is the first-line non-stimulant in adults
Baseline ECG, BP, HR mandatory before starting stimulants
State-specific authority prescription required for S8 stimulants
explanation.pearls: Atomoxetine takes 4-6 weeks to reach full effect; the dose ceiling in adults is 100 mg daily and the gain over stimulants is the absence of abuse liability and cardiac safety in patients with prior stimulant-induced events.

sources.label: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD
RANZCP Practice Guideline 23: Assessment and Treatment of ADHD

subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective vs bipolar with psychotic features
stem:
A 30-year-old woman is reviewed in a community psychiatry clinic with an established treating psychiatrist. She has had three episodes of significant mood disturbance over the last 8 years: two manic-like episodes with grandiose delusions and one major depressive episode with mood-congruent persecutory delusions. Between the second manic episode and the major depressive episode 18 months later, she had a continuous 12-week period of clear-cut auditory hallucinations and persecutory delusions without prominent mood symptoms; her mood was reported as euthymic by her partner and family throughout that interval.

She has been on olanzapine 15 mg nightly during episodes and has had remission of psychosis after each episode. She has not used substances. Family history includes a maternal aunt with schizophrenia and a paternal grandfather with bipolar I disorder. Current mental state is euthymic with no psychotic features. The clinic is considering revising her diagnostic formulation. Cognition is intact, no neurological signs.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78/min, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mental state - euthymic, no psychomotor change, no psychotic features, no suicidal ideation; cognition intact on MMSE 30 of 30
data_table.Investigations: Bloods - FBC, electrolytes, LFTs, TSH, vitamin B12 within normal limits; urine drug screen negative; CT brain (historical, 4 years ago) normal; MRI brain (historical, 18 months ago) normal

lead_in: Based on the longitudinal course described, what is the most likely DSM-5-TR diagnosis?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features in the most recent depressive episode and grandiose features in the prior manic episodes during the illness history
options.rationale: Bipolar I with psychotic features requires that psychotic symptoms only occur during mood episodes; the 12-week interval of psychosis in the absence of mood symptoms excludes this. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Schizophrenia and Related Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Schizophrenia and Related Disorders

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type with the lifetime presence of a major mood episode concurrent with criterion A schizophrenia symptoms and 2 or more weeks of psychotic symptoms without prominent mood symptoms
options.rationale: DSM-5-TR requires (a) a major mood episode (manic or major depressive) concurrent with criterion A of schizophrenia, (b) delusions or hallucinations for 2 or more weeks in the absence of a major mood episode, (c) mood symptoms present for the majority of total illness duration. All three are met. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophrenia with comorbid recurrent major depressive disorder and manic features attributable to substance use because the diagnosis of psychosis is supported by clear hallucinations
options.rationale: Schizophrenia with comorbid mood disorder requires mood symptoms to be present for a MINORITY of the total illness duration; her mood episodes are prominent and recurrent rather than residual. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder with affective features and a brief duration of mood symptoms within the context of a primary psychotic illness over the last 8 years of her psychiatric history
options.rationale: Schizophreniform is defined by total psychotic illness duration of less than 6 months; the 8 year course makes this diagnosis inapplicable. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Delusional disorder mixed type with secondary mood symptoms occurring intermittently and the longitudinal course best characterised by persistent encapsulated non-bizarre delusions
options.rationale: Delusional disorder requires criterion A delusions for 1 month or longer without prominent hallucinations or disorganised speech; this patient has clear hallucinations and mood episodes, which rules it out. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: DSM-5-TR criteria for schizoaffective disorder are: (A) an uninterrupted period of illness during which there is a major mood episode (major depressive or manic) concurrent with criterion A of schizophrenia (delusions, hallucinations, disorganised speech, grossly disorganised behaviour, or negative symptoms); (B) delusions or hallucinations for 2 or more weeks in the absence of a major mood episode during the lifetime duration of the illness; (C) mood-episode symptoms present for the majority of the total illness duration; (D) not attributable to a substance or medical condition. Bipolar type is subtyped when mania has occurred; depressive type when only major depressive episodes have occurred. Treatment combines antipsychotic with mood stabiliser or antidepressant (depending on subtype); long-term prognosis is intermediate between schizophrenia and bipolar disorder.
key_points: DSM-5-TR schizoaffective requires 2 or more weeks of psychosis without mood symptoms
Mood symptoms must be present for the MAJORITY of total illness duration
Bipolar type if mania ever occurred; depressive type if only MDD
Differential from bipolar with psychosis hinges on inter-episode psychosis-only periods
Treatment combines antipsychotic plus mood stabiliser (bipolar type) or antidepressant (depressive type)
explanation.pearls: The 2-week rule (psychosis without mood symptoms) is the key discriminator between schizoaffective disorder and bipolar disorder with psychotic features; missing this transition window causes most diagnostic errors in this differential.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Schizophrenia and Related Disorders

subtopic_detail: Serotonin syndrome with linezolid and SSRI
stem:
A 38-year-old man with chronic recurrent diabetic foot osteomyelitis is admitted to a metropolitan hospital for intravenous antibiotic therapy. He has been taking sertraline 100 mg daily for 6 years for major depressive disorder; mood is in remission. The infectious diseases team commenced intravenous linezolid 36 hours ago for MRSA cover after culture results returned. He was previously stable on oral sertraline through the admission process.

This morning he becomes agitated, confused, and hyperthermic. Nursing staff document inducible clonus in both lower limbs, hyperreflexia worse in the lower limbs than upper, mydriasis, diaphoresis, and a tremor. Sodium and bicarbonate are normal. There is no neck stiffness, no rash, no photophobia. He has had no sympathomimetic medications. The Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria are met. The medical team are calling for advice on management.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 138/min, BP 168/96 mmHg, RR 26/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 39.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Agitated and confused, lower-limb-dominant hyperreflexia and inducible clonus, mydriasis, diaphoresis, fine tremor; no neck stiffness; no rash
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 140 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, urea and creatinine within normal limits; CK 1820 U/L; venous blood gas pH 7.34, lactate 3.8 mmol/L (0.5-2.2); CT brain unremarkable

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management priority alongside ICU referral and active cooling?

options.text: Administer intravenous bromocriptine 2.5 mg three times daily as a dopaminergic rescue for the hyperthermic syndrome and continue the linezolid and sertraline at full dose throughout
options.rationale: Bromocriptine is used as dopaminergic rescue in neuroleptic malignant syndrome, not serotonin syndrome; the antidote concept here is cyproheptadine (5-HT2A antagonist), not a dopamine agonist. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Commence intravenous dantrolene 1 mg/kg as muscle relaxation for the hyperthermia and rigidity and continue both linezolid and sertraline because the infection requires ongoing treatment
options.rationale: Dantrolene is used in malignant hyperthermia and neuroleptic malignant syndrome; in serotonin toxicity, benzodiazepines are first-line. Continuing the precipitants is the major management error. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Switch linezolid to vancomycin and continue sertraline because vancomycin does not interact with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and infection treatment must continue
options.rationale: Vancomycin substitution is a sensible eventual antibiotic plan but the IMMEDIATE priority is to stop BOTH serotonergic agents and provide supportive care; sertraline cessation also matters because its half-life is long. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Stop linezolid and sertraline, give intravenous benzodiazepines for agitation and rigidity, and consider cyproheptadine 12 mg orally if features persist after first-line measures
options.rationale: Serotonin toxicity is a clinical diagnosis (Hunter criteria); treatment is removal of serotonergic agents, supportive care with benzodiazepines, active cooling, and cyproheptadine for refractory cases. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria; UpToDate)

options.text: Administer intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg slow injection for the agitation and confusion and continue current medications while waiting for the toxicology consultant review
options.rationale: Flumazenil is a benzodiazepine antagonist; agitation in serotonin syndrome benefits from benzodiazepines, NOT flumazenil. Flumazenil also lowers seizure threshold and worsens autonomic instability. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Linezolid is a weak reversible MAOI; co-prescription with SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, tramadol, fentanyl, pethidine, dextromethorphan, lithium, triptans, methylene blue, or recreational MDMA can precipitate serotonin syndrome. The Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria require ingestion of a serotonergic agent plus one of: spontaneous clonus, inducible clonus with agitation or diaphoresis, ocular clonus with agitation or diaphoresis, tremor with hyperreflexia, hypertonia plus temperature above 38 °C with ocular or inducible clonus. Treatment is removal of all serotonergic agents, benzodiazepines for agitation and clonus, active cooling, and cyproheptadine 12 mg orally followed by 2 mg every 2 hours (max 32 mg/day) for refractory cases. Severe cases require ICU and intubation with non-depolarising muscle relaxants. Recovery is usually within 24-72 hours.
key_points: Hunter criteria diagnose serotonin syndrome clinically
Linezolid is a weak reversible MAOI that combines dangerously with SSRIs
Stop ALL serotonergic agents, supportive care with benzodiazepines, active cooling
Cyproheptadine 12 mg orally for refractory cases
Bromocriptine and dantrolene are NMS, NOT serotonin toxicity, treatments
explanation.pearls: Lower-limb-dominant hyperreflexia, inducible clonus, mydriasis, and hyperthermia in the context of serotonergic co-prescription distinguish serotonin syndrome from NMS; antidote is cyproheptadine 12 mg PO, NOT bromocriptine or dantrolene.

subtopic_detail: Atrial fibrillation DOAC choice with renal impairment
stem:
A 78-year-old woman is reviewed by her GP for ongoing care of newly diagnosed non-valvular atrial fibrillation. She has a history of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and stage 3b chronic kidney disease. She weighs 58 kg. She has had no falls or significant bleeding. She lives independently with her husband and remains mobile. Her HAS-BLED score is 2 and her CHA2DS2-VASc score is 5 (age, hypertension, diabetes, female sex, T2DM).

She is currently on metformin 500 mg twice daily, perindopril 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, and gliclazide 60 mg modified release daily. She has no documented mitral stenosis, no mechanical valve, and her recent transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrated normal left ventricular function with mild mitral annular calcification. She is reviewed for choice of stroke prevention. Her eGFR has been stable at 32-34 mL/min/1.73m2 over the past 6 months.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88/min irregular, BP 138/82 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 97% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Irregularly irregular pulse, no carotid bruit, no lower limb oedema; weight 58 kg; mental state intact, normal gait, no recent falls
data_table.Investigations: ECG atrial fibrillation; creatinine 138 micromol/L (50-90), eGFR 32 mL/min/1.73m2; haemoglobin 124 g/L, platelets 198 x10^9/L; INR 1.0; transthoracic echo normal LV function with mild MAC; CHA2DS2-VASc 5, HAS-BLED 2

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line anticoagulation choice for this patient?

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily orally as direct oral anticoagulation for non-valvular atrial fibrillation given her CHA2DS2-VASc score is 5 and HAS-BLED is acceptable at 2
options.rationale: She meets 2 of the 3 dose-reduction criteria (weight 58 kg and creatinine 138 micromol/L); the correct dose is 2.5 mg twice daily, not the standard 5 mg twice daily. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily orally as direct oral anticoagulation for non-valvular atrial fibrillation given her renal function and age and weight profile
options.rationale: Apixaban reduced dose criteria require 2 of: age 80 or older, weight 60 kg or less, creatinine 133 micromol/L or higher; she meets weight and creatinine criteria, so 2.5 mg twice daily is correct. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular; AMH; NHFA/CSANZ Atrial Fibrillation Guideline)

options.text: Dabigatran 110 mg twice daily orally as direct oral anticoagulation for non-valvular atrial fibrillation given her chronic kidney disease and age and stable eGFR
options.rationale: Dabigatran is 80% renally cleared and is contraindicated in CrCl below 30 mL/min and used with caution in 30-50 mL/min; apixaban (less renal clearance) is preferred at her eGFR of 32. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Warfarin titrated to INR 2 to 3 with weekly monitoring during initiation and monthly INR monitoring thereafter via a community pharmacy or general practice clinic
options.rationale: Warfarin is reserved for mechanical valves, moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis, and ESRF on dialysis; DOACs are first-line in non-valvular AF including with stage 3b CKD per AU guidance. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Atrial Fibrillation Guideline)

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily as antiplatelet therapy for stroke prevention given her age and concerns about bleeding risk on full anticoagulation given her chronic kidney disease today
options.rationale: Aspirin is NOT recommended for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation - the benefit is small and bleeding risk approaches that of full anticoagulation. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Atrial Fibrillation Guideline)

explanation.summary: NHFA/CSANZ recommends direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) as first-line stroke prevention in non-valvular atrial fibrillation with CHA2DS2-VASc 2 or higher (women) or 1 or higher (men). Apixaban is preferred in elderly patients with renal impairment because of its lower renal clearance. The apixaban dose reduction criteria require 2 of: age 80 years or older, weight 60 kg or less, serum creatinine 133 micromol/L or higher. Dabigatran is contraindicated below CrCl 30 mL/min. Warfarin is reserved for mechanical valves and moderate-severe mitral stenosis. Aspirin is NOT recommended for stroke prevention. HAS-BLED informs bleeding-risk modification (BP control, alcohol, drugs) rather than withholding anticoagulation.
key_points: DOACs are first-line for non-valvular AF in Australia
Apixaban dose reduction: 2 of age 80+, weight 60 kg or less, creatinine 133+ micromol/L
Dabigatran contraindicated below CrCl 30 mL/min
Warfarin reserved for mechanical valves and moderate-severe MS
Aspirin not recommended for AF stroke prevention
explanation.pearls: Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily is the AU standard dose when any 2 of 3 criteria are met (age 80+, weight 60 kg or less, creatinine 133 micromol/L or higher); under-dosing without meeting criteria reduces stroke protection without lowering bleeding.

subtopic_detail: Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes immediate management
stem:
A 67-year-old man with stage 4 CKD attends the emergency department with one day of muscle weakness and palpitations. His regular medications include enalapril 20 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, frusemide 40 mg daily, and a recent 5 day course of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole started 4 days ago by his GP for a presumed UTI. He has not changed his diet but his urine output has decreased over the last 48 hours.

On arrival he is alert but feels generally weak. Bedside ECG shows sinus rhythm at 56/min with peaked T waves in the chest leads, broadened QRS at 124 ms, and absent P waves in lead II. A point-of-care blood gas confirms severe hyperkalaemia. Calcium gluconate has been drawn up by the nurse. He has been put on continuous cardiac monitoring and 2 large-bore intravenous cannulae have been sited. He is haemodynamically stable. There is no chest pain.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 56/min, BP 132/78 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, generalised weakness, no focal neurology, no ascites; no pitting oedema; abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Investigations: Point of care VBG potassium 7.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), sodium 132 mmol/L, pH 7.28, bicarbonate 16 mmol/L; creatinine 396 micromol/L (baseline 220), eGFR 14 mL/min/1.73m2; venous glucose 6.4 mmol/L; ECG sinus bradycardia 56/min, peaked T waves V2-V5, QRS 124 ms, absent P waves lead II

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate next intervention?

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 10 mg via mask in 4 mL of normal saline as a beta-2 agonist to shift potassium intracellularly via stimulation of the sodium potassium ATPase pump
options.rationale: Nebulised salbutamol is an adjunct to insulin-dextrose for potassium shifting but is not the first step when ECG changes are present; membrane stabilisation precedes shifting. (Source: UpToDate Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Intravenous insulin 10 units in 50 mL of 50% dextrose over 15 minutes to shift potassium intracellularly with bedside glucose monitoring every 30 minutes for 4 hours
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose shifts potassium effectively but takes 15-30 minutes to start working; calcium gluconate must precede it when ECG changes are present to stabilise the myocyte membrane. (Source: UpToDate Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL slow push over 2 minutes to stabilise the cardiac myocyte membrane with cardiac monitoring during administration of the bolus
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemia with broadened QRS and peaked T waves requires immediate myocyte membrane stabilisation with calcium gluconate before insulin-dextrose; calcium stabilises within 1-3 minutes and lasts 30-60 minutes. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology; UpToDate Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Resonium) 30 g now and repeat every 4 hours until normokalaemia is achieved with daily monitoring of his electrolytes and renal function
options.rationale: Resonium removes potassium slowly (hours) and has GI side effects including necrosis; it has no role in immediate management of life-threatening hyperkalaemia with ECG changes. (Source: UpToDate Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Urgent renal replacement therapy via temporary central venous access at the nearest tertiary centre and ICU transfer with nephrology consultation before any pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Dialysis is the definitive treatment in refractory hyperkalaemia or CKD/AKI without rapid response; immediate calcium gluconate plus insulin-dextrose precedes dialysis preparation and bridges to RRT. (Source: UpToDate Hyperkalaemia)

explanation.summary: Hyperkalaemia management proceeds in 3 sequential steps when ECG changes are present: (1) membrane stabilisation with calcium gluconate (10 mL 10% IV over 2 minutes; works in 1-3 min, lasts 30-60 min); (2) intracellular shift with insulin 10 units in 50 mL of 50% dextrose IV over 15 min plus nebulised salbutamol 10 mg plus sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol IV (if metabolic acidosis); (3) total body removal via loop diuretic (with intact kidney function), patiromer or zirconium cyclosilicate, or dialysis. Identify and stop the precipitant: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, spironolactone, NSAIDs, trimethoprim, beta-blockers, heparin. Calcium chloride is more potent than calcium gluconate but requires central access.
key_points: Sequence: stabilise (calcium), shift (insulin-dextrose, salbutamol, bicarbonate), remove (loop, binder, dialysis)
Calcium gluconate works within 1-3 minutes and lasts 30-60 minutes
Trimethoprim plus ACE inhibitor plus spironolactone is a classical pharmacological triad of hyperkalaemia
Resonium and patiromer are slow GI removers (hours), not for immediate use
Dialysis is definitive in refractory or CKD/AKI cases
explanation.pearls: Trimethoprim added to background ACE inhibitor plus spironolactone in CKD is a classical hyperkalaemia precipitant; the trimethoprim blocks the renal tubular ENaC channel similarly to amiloride and can raise serum potassium by 1-2 mmol/L within days.

subtopic_detail: Severe community acquired pneumonia AU empirical antibiotics
stem:
A 58-year-old man presents to a metropolitan emergency department with 3 days of progressively worsening productive cough, pleuritic right-sided chest pain, fever, and breathlessness. He is normally well, drinks 20 standard drinks per week, and smokes 15 cigarettes per day. He has not travelled recently and has no specific occupational exposures. He has no penicillin allergy.

On arrival he is alert but tachypnoeic with confusion to time on the AMTS (8 of 10). Bronchial breathing is heard at the right base with reduced expansion. Crackles extend up to mid zone. There is no peripheral oedema. A chest x-ray shows right middle and lower lobe consolidation with a small effusion. His SMART-COP score is calculated by the registrar including systolic blood pressure 88 mmHg, multilobar involvement, low albumin, respiratory rate 32, tachycardia 122, and confusion. He has had peripheral intravenous access established and 2 sets of blood cultures and a sputum sample have been taken.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 122/min, BP 88/56 mmHg, RR 32/min, SpO2 91% room air, temperature 39.1 °C
data_table.Examination: Bronchial breath sounds right base extending to mid zone with reduced expansion; small right pleural effusion; capillary refill 3 seconds; abbreviated mental test score 8 of 10
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.4 x10^9/L with neutrophilia, CRP 312 mg/L (<5), urea 12.6 mmol/L (2.5-7.5), albumin 28 g/L (35-50), lactate 3.6 mmol/L (0.5-2.2); SMART-COP score 6 (very high); chest x-ray right middle/lower lobe consolidation with small effusion; blood cultures and sputum sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical intravenous antibiotic regimen to commence now?

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.2 g 6-hourly and intravenous doxycycline 100 mg 12-hourly until microbiological results return and severity is reassessed
options.rationale: This is the standard moderate severity AU regimen; with SMART-COP 6 (severe) the recommended Tx Guidelines pathway uses ceftriaxone plus azithromycin (or moxifloxacin monotherapy if beta-lactam allergic). (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 1 g daily and intravenous azithromycin 500 mg daily as empirical cover for severe community acquired pneumonia per Therapeutic Guidelines
options.rationale: SMART-COP 5 or higher is severe CAP; empirical IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily PLUS IV azithromycin 500 mg daily is the AU Therapeutic Guidelines first-line for severe CAP, covering S pneumoniae and atypical organisms. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g 6-hourly and intravenous vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading dose as broad spectrum cover for severe pneumonia with possible MRSA
options.rationale: Pip-taz plus vancomycin is reserved for healthcare-associated or aspiration pneumonia or post-influenza pneumonia where Pseudomonas or MRSA are suspected; not standard CAP cover. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous moxifloxacin 400 mg daily as monotherapy targeting Streptococcus pneumoniae and atypical pathogens together with a single agent in severe community acquired pneumonia
options.rationale: Moxifloxacin monotherapy is the AU alternative ONLY for severe beta-lactam allergy; in non-allergic patients ceftriaxone plus azithromycin is preferred and has better antimicrobial stewardship. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g 8-hourly plus oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 5 days outpatient with telephone review in 48 hours from the GP for symptom progression
options.rationale: Oral outpatient therapy is the AU regimen for low-severity CAP (CRB-65 0 or SMART-COP 0-2); SMART-COP 6 is severe and requires IV antibiotics with admission and HDU/ICU review. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: SMART-COP severity tool (Systolic BP, Multilobar, Albumin, Respiratory rate, Tachycardia, Confusion, Oxygen, pH) stratifies CAP. A score of 0-2 indicates outpatient management with oral antibiotics; 3-4 indicates admission for moderate disease; 5 or higher indicates severe CAP requiring IV antibiotics, HDU/ICU consideration, and assessment for invasive ventilation or vasopressors. AU Therapeutic Guidelines first-line for severe CAP is ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily plus azithromycin 500 mg IV daily, escalating to a wider regimen (add gentamicin or pip-taz) for septic shock, immunocompromise, or specific exposures. Moxifloxacin monotherapy is the alternative in severe beta-lactam allergy.
key_points: SMART-COP 5+ is severe CAP requiring IV antibiotics and HDU/ICU assessment
AU first-line severe CAP: ceftriaxone 1 g + azithromycin 500 mg IV daily
Moxifloxacin monotherapy only for severe beta-lactam allergy
Pip-taz plus vancomycin reserved for HAP, aspiration, post-influenza
Atypical organisms (Legionella, Mycoplasma) covered by macrolide
explanation.pearls: SMART-COP score of 5 or higher predicts a need for intensive respiratory or vasopressor support in CAP and triggers IV ceftriaxone PLUS IV azithromycin; the macrolide cover for Legionella is the change from moderate-severity therapy.

sources.label: TSANZ/ATS Guidelines for the Management of Community-Acquired Pneumonia

stem:
A 72-year-old man with stage 3a CKD, type 2 diabetes, and stable angina is admitted with new exertional chest pain. He is taking metformin 1 g twice daily, ramipril 10 mg daily, frusemide 40 mg daily, perindopril, atorvastatin, and a recent course of ibuprofen for back pain. Troponin is mildly elevated and he has been booked for invasive coronary angiography in the morning.

He is haemodynamically stable. He is on a low background of oxygen 2 L/min for slight hypoxia. The cardiology team have asked you to prepare him for the procedure. His baseline creatinine is 138 micromol/L and his estimated GFR is 42 mL/min/1.73m2. Last echocardiogram showed normal LV function. He has had no recent diarrhoea, vomiting, or oral intake change. The procedure is non-emergent and could be delayed 24 hours if clinically warranted to optimise his renal status before exposure to iodinated contrast.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78/min, BP 138/82 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 96% on 2 L/min oxygen, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mucous membranes moist, no oedema; capillary refill 2 seconds; no focal cardiac findings beyond mild gallop rhythm; no jugular venous distension
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.4 mmol/L, urea 9.6 mmol/L (2.5-7.5), creatinine 138 micromol/L (50-90), eGFR 42 mL/min/1.73m2; troponin I 78 ng/L (<14, normal less than 14); haemoglobin 124 g/L; HbA1c 7.8% (5.6-6.4%)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate prophylactic intervention to reduce his risk of contrast-induced acute kidney injury?

options.text: Intravenous isotonic 0.9% sodium chloride at 1 mL/kg/hour for 12 hours pre-procedure and 12 hours post-procedure with monitoring of fluid status and urine output
options.rationale: Isotonic IV crystalloid pre- and post-contrast is the only AU-recommended pharmacological prophylaxis with consistent evidence; KHA-CARI and KDIGO recommend 1 mL/kg/hour for 12 hours pre- and 12 hours post-procedure. (Source: KHA-CARI Guideline: Use of Contrast Agents in Patients with CKD; KDIGO AKI)
source_refs: KHA-CARI Guideline: Use of Contrast Agents in Patients with CKD

options.text: Oral N-acetylcysteine 600 mg twice daily for 24 hours pre-procedure and 24 hours post-procedure as a pharmacological prophylaxis for contrast-induced acute kidney injury
options.rationale: The PRESERVE trial (NEJM 2018) showed no benefit from N-acetylcysteine for contrast-induced AKI prevention versus placebo; AU and international guidelines no longer recommend it. (Source: PRESERVE trial; KDIGO AKI)

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 154 mmol/L at 3 mL/kg/hour for 1 hour pre-procedure and 1 mL/kg/hour for 6 hours post-procedure for renal protection against contrast nephropathy
options.rationale: PRESERVE trial also showed no benefit from sodium bicarbonate over isotonic saline; the simpler regimen of 0.9% saline is now standard. (Source: PRESERVE trial; KDIGO AKI)

options.text: Withhold metformin for 48 hours pre-procedure and 48 hours post-procedure to reduce the risk of lactic acidosis associated with metformin retention after iodinated contrast exposure
options.rationale: Withholding metformin reduces lactic acidosis risk (separate concern from CIN) but does NOT prevent contrast-induced acute kidney injury; standard practice is to withhold metformin in eGFR less than 30 OR check creatinine 48-72 h post-contrast in moderate CKD. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Pre-procedure furosemide 20 mg intravenously and mannitol 100 mL of 20% solution to maintain urine output during the procedure and post-procedure to reduce contrast nephropathy
options.rationale: Forced diuresis with furosemide and mannitol INCREASES contrast-induced AKI risk by reducing intravascular volume; isotonic hydration alone is the only proven prophylactic intervention. (Source: KDIGO AKI)

explanation.summary: Contrast-induced acute kidney injury (CI-AKI) risk rises with CKD, diabetes, heart failure, advanced age, hypovolaemia, repeat contrast exposure, NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors/ARBs at high doses, and high-volume or hyperosmolar contrast. KDIGO and KHA-CARI recommend isotonic saline IV (1 mL/kg/hour for 12 hours pre- and 12 hours post-contrast) as the only evidence-based pharmacological prophylaxis. The PRESERVE trial definitively refuted N-acetylcysteine and sodium bicarbonate. Adjuncts include: minimise contrast volume, use iso-osmolar or low-osmolar contrast, stop nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides), and hold metformin if eGFR less than 30 (or recheck creatinine 48-72 h post-contrast at moderate CKD). Statins have a small protective effect.
key_points: Isotonic saline 1 mL/kg/hour for 12h pre- and 12h post-contrast
PRESERVE trial: no benefit from N-acetylcysteine or bicarbonate
Stop nephrotoxins (NSAIDs) pre-procedure
Withhold metformin only for lactic acidosis concern, not CIN prevention
Forced diuresis worsens CIN risk
explanation.pearls: Isotonic 0.9% saline 1 mL/kg/hour for 12 hours pre- and 12 hours post-contrast is the only proven CIN prophylaxis; PRESERVE trial 2018 ended the use of N-acetylcysteine and sodium bicarbonate.

sources.label: KHA-CARI Guideline: Use of Contrast Agents in Patients with CKD

subtopic_detail: Adrenal crisis precipitated by intercurrent illness
stem:
A 41-year-old man with primary adrenal insufficiency (autoimmune Addison disease, diagnosed 6 years ago) attends a metropolitan emergency department brought in by his wife. He has had 2 days of fever, profuse vomiting, and watery diarrhoea after a barbecue. He stopped his oral hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone 36 hours ago because of the vomiting. He has lost approximately 4 kg in body weight over this period and now feels too weak to stand.

On arrival he is lethargic but rousable. His mucous membranes are dry, capillary refill is 4 seconds, and he has flat neck veins on examination. There is a faint diffuse hyperpigmentation of the buccal mucosa and palmar creases. There is no abdominal tenderness or peritonism. He has known hypothyroidism on thyroxine 100 mcg daily. His usual hydrocortisone dose is 15 mg morning and 5 mg afternoon. He carries an emergency hydrocortisone vial but has not used it. He has not had any recent steroid dose adjustment.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 132/min, BP 84/52 mmHg, RR 24/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 38.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, dry mucous membranes, flat neck veins, capillary refill 4 seconds; buccal and palmar crease hyperpigmentation; abdomen soft non-tender; no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 122 mmol/L, potassium 6.0 mmol/L, urea 14.8 mmol/L (2.5-7.5), creatinine 168 micromol/L (baseline 88); venous blood gas pH 7.30, bicarbonate 16 mmol/L, lactate 3.4 mmol/L (0.5-2.2); venous glucose 3.2 mmol/L; cortisol pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate single best immediate intervention?

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus immediately followed by 200 mg over 24 hours by continuous infusion or 50 mg 6-hourly with intravenous 0.9% saline resuscitation
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus is the single most important intervention in adrenal crisis; do not wait for cortisol results. Saline 1 L over 1 hour for hypovolaemia; dextrose for hypoglycaemia; treat precipitant. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis and Treatment of Primary Adrenal Insufficiency; Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency

options.text: Intravenous dexamethasone 4 mg now to provide glucocorticoid replacement and allow the planned short synacthen test to remain interpretable for diagnostic confirmation
options.rationale: Dexamethasone is preferred when the diagnosis is uncertain to preserve cortisol assay; this patient has known established Addison disease so hydrocortisone is the correct agent and starts within minutes. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency

options.text: Intravenous fludrocortisone equivalent 1 mg orally or via nasogastric tube alongside aggressive fluid resuscitation with intravenous 0.9% saline at 1 L over the first hour
options.rationale: Fludrocortisone is not required acutely because high-dose hydrocortisone (>50 mg/day) has sufficient mineralocorticoid activity; oral fludrocortisone is added when daily steroid drops below 50 mg. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency

options.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL slow push for hyperkalaemia and then insulin-dextrose to shift potassium followed by intravenous hydrocortisone replacement at standard maintenance dose
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemia in adrenal crisis resolves with cortisol replacement and saline; calcium gluconate is only indicated if ECG changes present. Hydrocortisone bolus is the priority and corrects the underlying cause. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency

options.text: Take blood and urine cultures and commence intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g empirically as the most likely cause of his decompensation given the fever and gastrointestinal infection symptoms today
options.rationale: Source-control and empirical antibiotics are appropriate but the immediate priority is glucocorticoid replacement; sepsis without cortisol replacement in Addison disease is rapidly fatal. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis is a medical emergency precipitated by infection, gastroenteritis (vomiting prevents oral steroid absorption), trauma, surgery, or sudden cessation of glucocorticoid. Classical presentation: hypotension, dehydration, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, fever, abdominal pain. Treatment is hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus immediately, followed by 200 mg over 24 hours (or 50 mg every 6 hours), plus IV 0.9% saline 1 L over 1 hour for hypovolaemia, dextrose for hypoglycaemia, treat precipitant, and continue at stress dose for 24-48 hours before titrating down. Mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone) is added when daily glucocorticoid drops below 50 mg because hydrocortisone has intrinsic mineralocorticoid activity at high doses. Education on sick-day rules (double oral dose for febrile illness, IM hydrocortisone 100 mg for vomiting) is the cornerstone of prevention.
key_points: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus is the priority intervention
Followed by 200 mg/24 h infusion or 50 mg IV 6-hourly
Saline 1 L over 1 hour for hypovolaemia, dextrose for hypoglycaemia
Fludrocortisone added only when daily steroid is below 50 mg
Sick-day rules: double oral dose for fever, IM hydrocortisone for vomiting
explanation.pearls: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV is the immediate priority in adrenal crisis; mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone) is only added when total daily hydrocortisone drops below 50 mg because high-dose hydrocortisone has sufficient mineralocorticoid activity at the kidney.

sources.label: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency

subtopic_detail: Sgarbossa criteria in chest pain with new LBBB
stem:
A 64-year-old woman with hypertension and dyslipidaemia presents to a regional emergency department by ambulance with 80 minutes of severe central chest pain radiating to her left jaw and arm. She is sweaty and nauseated. There is no syncope and no cyanosis. She has a baseline ECG from 18 months ago in her electronic record which showed sinus rhythm with a normal QRS axis and no bundle branch block.

On arrival she is alert but in distress with the pain. Her bedside ECG shows sinus rhythm with new left bundle branch block; in V1-V3 there is 3 mm concordant ST elevation, in lead III there is 2 mm discordant ST elevation, and in V6 there is 5 mm discordant ST depression. Her chest is clear; cardiovascular examination is otherwise unremarkable. There is no peripheral oedema. A point of care troponin returns at 22 ng/L (less than 14). The nearest PCI-capable centre is 80 minutes by road. She has been given aspirin 300 mg orally and clopidogrel 600 mg orally in the ambulance.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96/min, BP 142/86 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 97% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Pain at rest, diaphoretic; chest clear, dual heart sounds with soft S4 gallop; no peripheral oedema; capillary refill 2 seconds; carotid pulses equal and full
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm with new LBBB, V1-V3 with 3 mm concordant ST elevation, lead III with 2 mm discordant ST elevation, V6 with 5 mm discordant ST depression; troponin I 22 ng/L (<14); haemoglobin 134 g/L; chest x-ray clear; baseline ECG (18 months ago) sinus rhythm no BBB

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Repeat the ECG in 30 minutes after analgesia and another point of care troponin and only escalate to reperfusion therapy if the second troponin returns elevated above the threshold
options.rationale: Waiting for serial troponin or repeat ECG in a STEMI-equivalent loses myocardium; positive Sgarbossa criteria with new LBBB warrant immediate reperfusion. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ ACS Guideline 2016)

options.text: Arrange direct transfer to the nearest percutaneous coronary intervention capable centre for primary PCI within 120 minutes from first medical contact regardless of the LBBB ECG findings
options.rationale: Primary PCI is preferred when transfer time is under 90-120 minutes from first medical contact; with 80 minutes transfer plus angiography prep time the total exceeds the 120 minute window and thrombolysis is preferred. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ ACS Guideline 2016)

options.text: Commence intravenous heparin 60 units/kg loading and 12 units/kg/hour infusion plus high-dose statin pending serial 3 hour troponin and admit to the coronary care unit for medical management
options.rationale: Treating this as NSTEMI ignores positive Sgarbossa with new LBBB; classifying it as a STEMI-equivalent is the standard of care and time-to-reperfusion drives outcome. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ ACS Guideline 2016)

options.text: Administer thrombolysis with tenecteplase based on weight and reassess for transfer to a PCI-capable centre if symptoms persist or ECG fails to improve within 60 to 90 minutes
options.rationale: Modified Sgarbossa criteria are positive (concordant ST elevation 1 mm or more, OR concordant ST depression 1 mm or more in V1-V3, OR excessive discordant ST elevation); new LBBB plus positive Sgarbossa with 80 minute PCI transfer is a STEMI equivalent and requires thrombolysis. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Acute Coronary Syndromes 2016; Sgarbossa criteria)

options.text: Administer intravenous glyceryl trinitrate infusion and intravenous morphine for pain relief and observe the ECG response while transferring her to the cardiology ward for further evaluation
options.rationale: Analgesia and GTN are appropriate adjuncts but do not address the time-critical reperfusion need; symptomatic management without reperfusion is a major error in suspected STEMI. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ ACS Guideline 2016)

explanation.summary: New left bundle branch block in the setting of chest pain is not automatically a STEMI equivalent; the modified Sgarbossa criteria (Smith) identify which patients have acute coronary occlusion. Positive criteria are: (1) concordant ST elevation 1 mm or more in any lead; (2) concordant ST depression 1 mm or more in V1, V2, or V3; (3) discordant ST elevation with proportionally excessive elevation (ST/S ratio of -0.25 or worse). Any one positive feature classifies as STEMI equivalent. Reperfusion strategy in Australia: primary PCI if first medical contact to balloon time is 90-120 minutes or less; otherwise fibrinolysis with tenecteplase, then transfer for rescue PCI if no resolution in 60-90 minutes (pharmaco-invasive strategy). Time-to-reperfusion is the dominant predictor of outcome.
key_points: New LBBB is not automatically a STEMI equivalent
Modified Sgarbossa criteria identify acute coronary occlusion in LBBB
Concordant ST elevation 1 mm or more is the most specific criterion
Reperfusion: primary PCI if under 120 min, otherwise thrombolysis plus transfer
Pharmaco-invasive strategy: thrombolysis then rescue PCI if no resolution
explanation.pearls: Modified Sgarbossa criteria - concordant ST elevation 1 mm or more in any lead is the most specific criterion (over 90%) for acute coronary occlusion in new LBBB; this is the key discriminator that drives the reperfusion decision.

stem:
A 27-year-old woman presents to a metropolitan emergency department 26 hours after the start of a staggered paracetamol ingestion. She began taking the tablets at 6 pm yesterday and continued at intervals over an 11-hour period, finishing at 5 am today. She has taken her own count of 24 x 500 mg tablets (total 12 g over 11 hours). She has been crying and nauseated since the morning. She drinks heavily on weekends, around 6 standard drinks per night, and takes regular oral contraceptive pill.

She is alert and oriented. She has mild right upper quadrant tenderness but no peritonism. She is haemodynamically stable. Bedside venous blood gas shows a mild metabolic acidosis. She has no other co-ingestants on history; urine drug screen is pending. She admits to ongoing suicidal ideation. The toxicology service has been contacted by phone. Mental health team have been notified. Her weight is 65 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92/min, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert oriented; mild right upper quadrant tenderness; no peritonism; no asterixis, no jaundice; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol level 95 mg/L (taken on arrival); ALT 1280 U/L (<35), AST 1140 U/L, INR 2.1, creatinine 88 micromol/L, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L, lactate 3.2 mmol/L (0.5-2.2); venous blood gas pH 7.32; urine bhCG negative; weight 65 kg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Do not start acetylcysteine because the paracetamol nomogram cannot be applied to staggered ingestions and the level falls below the standard 4 hour treatment line cut off
options.rationale: The nomogram does not apply to staggered ingestion but acetylcysteine is started anyway if the cumulative dose exceeds 200 mg/kg or 10 g (whichever is less) in 24 hours OR if there are signs of hepatotoxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; AU Paracetamol Poisoning Guideline 2020)

options.text: Arrange urgent liver transplantation assessment based on King's College Hospital criteria before commencing acetylcysteine to facilitate listing and avoid delay if she meets transplant criteria today
options.rationale: Acetylcysteine is started immediately; King's College criteria are reassessed at 48-72 hours and beyond, not before initial antidote. Acetylcysteine plus transplant referral run in parallel, not sequentially. (Source: AU Paracetamol Poisoning Guideline 2020)

options.text: Administer single dose oral activated charcoal 50 g and observe for 4 hours with repeat paracetamol level to plot on the nomogram before commencing further definitive antidotal treatment
options.rationale: Charcoal is only useful within 2 hours of acute ingestion; this presentation is 26 hours after the start of ingestion. Nomogram does not apply to staggered ingestion. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Discharge the patient with referral to community mental health team and the GP for further follow up since the paracetamol level of 95 mg/L is below the treatment threshold for acute single ingestion
options.rationale: Active hepatotoxicity (ALT 1280, INR 2.1) and metabolic acidosis (lactate 3.2, bicarbonate 18) indicate severe paracetamol toxicity in progress and discharge would be catastrophic. (Source: AU Paracetamol Poisoning Guideline 2020)

options.text: Commence intravenous acetylcysteine using the 2 bag regimen with 200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours and refer to hepatology for King's College Hospital criteria assessment
options.rationale: Staggered ingestion exceeding 200 mg/kg in 24 hours plus evidence of hepatotoxicity (ALT 1280, INR 2.1) mandates IV acetylcysteine using the 2-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours); King's College criteria triage for liver transplantation. (Source: AU Paracetamol Poisoning Guideline 2020)

explanation.summary: Australian paracetamol poisoning guideline 2020 distinguishes acute single ingestion (use Rumack-Matthew nomogram from 4 hour level) from staggered ingestion (ingestion over more than 1 hour) and repeated supratherapeutic ingestion. Staggered ingestion thresholds: 200 mg/kg or 10 g (whichever is less) in 24 hours, or 150 mg/kg over 48 hours, or 100 mg/kg daily over 72 hours. The 2-bag IV acetylcysteine regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours) has replaced the older 3-bag regimen due to fewer anaphylactoid reactions. Indications for continued infusion past 20 hours include rising ALT, INR over 2, lactic acidosis, or ongoing detectable paracetamol. King's College criteria (arterial pH less than 7.30 after fluid resuscitation; OR all of: PT over 100 seconds, creatinine over 300 micromol/L, grade 3-4 hepatic encephalopathy) trigger liver transplantation discussion.
key_points: Staggered paracetamol ingestion: do not use nomogram
Threshold: 200 mg/kg or 10 g in 24 hours plus hepatotoxic features
AU 2-bag IV acetylcysteine regimen replaces older 3-bag
Continued infusion if ALT rising, INR over 2, lactic acidosis, paracetamol detected
King's College criteria trigger transplant discussion
explanation.pearls: The 2-bag intravenous acetylcysteine regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours) has replaced the older 3-bag regimen because it gives the same dose with fewer anaphylactoid reactions; AU practice since 2020.
